Thirdly, utilizing Object Lifecycle Management (step 3) to move logs to a Coldline Storage bucket after 30 days addresses the cost aspect of long-term storage. Coldline Storage is significantly cheaper than standard storage, suitable for infrequently accessed data like older logs. Finally, configuring a retention policy at the bucket level with bucket lock (step 4) fulfills the compliance requirement of retaining logs for at least two years and prevents accidental deletion of the data.
Option B is suboptimal because it involves manually managing logs with a cron job. This increases operational overhead and potential for errors compared to the automated approach offered by Cloud Logging. Options C and D store the data into BigQuery, which is not cost-optimal when the primary requirement after 30 days is just to retain logs for audit and not for analysis. Option C does not offer any method to adhere to the required two-year retention and option D involves running cron jobs on all instances which is not an ideal approach compared to using Cloud Logging agents.
Therefore, option A achieves the requirements of compliance, cost-effectiveness, and adherence to Google- recommended practices.
Authoritative Links:
Cloud Logging Agent: https://cloud.google.com/logging/docs/agent Cloud Storage Sinks: https://cloud.google.com/logging/docs/export/configure_sinks Cloud Storage Object Lifecycle Management: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/lifecycle Cloud Storage Retention Policies: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/bucket-lock Cloud Storage Storage Classes: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/storage-classes

question: Your company has just recently activated Cloud Identity to manage users. The Google Cloud Organization has been configured as well. The security team needs to secure projects that will be part of the Organization. They want to prohibit IAM users outside the domain from gaining permissions from now on. What should they do?
options: Configure an organization policy to restrict identities by domain.
Configure an organization policy to block creation of service accounts.
Configure Cloud Scheduler to trigger a Cloud Function every hour that removes all users that don't belong to the Cloud Identity domain from all projects.

Create a technical user (e.g., [email protected]), and give it the project owner role at root organization level. Write a bash script that: ¢ Lists all the IAM rules of all projects within the organization. ¢ Deletes all users that do not belong to the company domain. Create a Compute Engine instance in a project within the Organization and configure gcloud to be executed with technical user credentials. Configure a cron job that executes the bash script every hour.
explanation:
Option A, configuring an organization policy to restrict identities by domain, is the most effective and appropriate solution. Organization policies provide centralized control and governance over your Google Cloud resources. Specifically, the "constraints/iam.allowedPolicyMemberDomains" constraint allows you to specify which domains are permitted to be granted IAM roles within the organization. By setting this policy, you can directly prohibit users from outside your Cloud Identity domain from being added to projects, preventing unauthorized access right from the point of configuration. This approach is proactive, automated, and doesn't rely on reactive scripts or scheduled jobs. It aligns with the principle of least privilege and provides consistent enforcement across the entire organization. Option B, blocking service account creation, is not the required action since the question was specifically about user identities, not service accounts.
Option C and D, while technically feasible, are cumbersome and reactive rather than preventative. They would incur more operational overhead, be less secure, and are less scalable. Moreover, scripts could encounter race conditions if users are being added and removed simultaneously. Choosing a policy-based solution leverages the inherent capabilities of Google Cloud for centralized access control, ensuring compliance and enhancing security. Organization policies are the recommended way to manage access control at scale.
Further research:
Organization policy constraints Using constraints Understanding organization policies

question: Your company has an application running on Google Cloud that is collecting data from thousands of physical devices that are globally distributed. Data is published to Pub/Sub and streamed in real time into an SSD Cloud Bigtable cluster via a Dataflow pipeline. The operations team informs you that your Cloud Bigtable cluster has a hotspot, and queries are taking longer than expected. You need to resolve the problem and prevent it from happening in the future. What should you do?
options: Advise your clients to use HBase APIs instead of NodeJS APIs.
Delete records older than 30 days.
Review your RowKey strategy and ensure that keys are evenly spread across the alphabet.
Double the number of nodes you currently have.
explanation:
The correct answer is C. Review your RowKey strategy and ensure that keys are evenly spread across the alphabet.
Here's why: Hotspots in Cloud Bigtable typically arise when writes are concentrated on a small set of rows or regions. This happens when row keys are not properly distributed. In this scenario, the data coming from thousands of globally distributed physical devices might be using sequential or time-based row keys, causing all writes to gravitate towards the same region of the Bigtable cluster. Consequently, this specific region becomes overloaded, leading to performance bottlenecks, i.e., a hotspot.
Option C directly addresses the root cause of hotspots by recommending a review of the row key strategy.
Ensuring that keys are evenly distributed across the alphabet (or any other large and diverse character set), effectively spreads the writes and reads across different regions in Bigtable, preventing a single region from becoming overloaded and thus resolving the hotspot issue. Using a well-distributed row key strategy is fundamental for optimal performance with Bigtable.
Option A (using HBase APIs) is irrelevant, as the problem is not API-related but rather data layout. Option B (deleting old records) can help with storage space but does not address the hotspot caused by poor row key design. Option D (doubling nodes) might temporarily alleviate the problem but doesn't fix the underlying issue of uneven data distribution; it’s an expensive band-aid, not a long-term solution. Row key optimization is crucial for efficient data processing within a distributed system such as Bigtable.For further reading:
Cloud Bigtable Schema Design: https://cloud.google.com/bigtable/docs/schema-design Key Visualizer: https://cloud.google.com/bigtable/docs/key-visualizer Avoiding Hotspots: https://cloud.google.com/bigtable/docs/avoiding-hotspots

question:
Your company has a Google Cloud project that uses BigQuery for data warehousing. There are some tables that contain personally identifiable information (PII).
Only the compliance team may access the PII. The other information in the tables must be available to the data science team. You want to minimize cost and the time it takes to assign appropriate access to the tables. What should you do?
options: 1. From the dataset where you have the source data, create views of tables that you want to share, excluding PII. 2. Assign an appropriate project-level IAM role to the members of the data science team. 3. Assign access controls to the dataset that contains the view.

1. From the dataset where you have the source data, create materialized views of tables that you want to share, excluding PII. 2. Assign an appropriate project-level IAM role to the members of the data science team. 3. Assign access controls to the dataset that contains the view.

1. Create a dataset for the data science team. 2. Create views of tables that you want to share, excluding PII. 3. Assign an appropriate project-level IAM role to the members of the data science team. 4. Assign access controls to the dataset that contains the view. 5. Authorize the view to access the source dataset.

1. Create a dataset for the data science team. 2. Create materialized views of tables that you want to share, excluding PII. 3. Assign an appropriate project-level IAM role to the members of the data science team. 4. Assign access controls to the dataset that contains the view. 5. Authorize the view to access the source dataset.
explanation:
Option C is the most appropriate solution because it effectively addresses the requirements of separating access to PII while minimizing cost and management overhead. Here's a breakdown:
1. Creating a separate dataset for the data science team (Step 1) ensures clear segregation of resources and simplifies access control management. It allows you to grant specific permissions at the dataset level without impacting other data assets.
2. Creating views (Step 2) rather than materialized views is crucial for cost minimization. Views are logical representations of data, not physical copies. They don't incur storage costs and always reflect the latest data in the base tables.
3. Excluding PII in the views ensures that data scientists only access the information relevant to their work, maintaining compliance with data privacy regulations.
4. Assigning an appropriate project-level IAM role (Step 3) gives the data science team the necessary access to the project as a whole. This enables them to use BigQuery and query the views.
5. Assigning access controls to the view dataset (Step 4) isolates the access to those views only to the members of the data science team and nobody else, this ensures clear access boundaries.
6. Authorizing the view to access the source dataset (Step 5) is essential because the view, which resides in a different dataset, needs permission to read data from the source dataset containing PII.
BigQuery views do not inherit permissions from source tables and datasets.
Options A and B don’t segregate access to data, meaning there could be cross access by members of the data science team. Option D is not recommended because materialized views are physical copies of data, incurring storage costs, maintenance overheads and possible data staleness when updates to the underlying source table occur. The choice of views keeps costs and effort to a minimum.
Relevant Cloud Computing Concepts:
Principle of Least Privilege: Option C follows this principle by granting only the necessary access to each team. The data science team does not require access to PII; therefore, they should be limited to data views with no PII included.
IAM (Identity and Access Management): IAM allows fine-grained control of access to GCP resources based on roles and permissions. This is central to managing access and security in BigQuery.
Data Governance: Separating access to PII is a core aspect of data governance. Views help to enforce data governance policies by creating a controlled view of the underlying data.
Cost Optimization: Views incur little cost compared to materialized views as they do not require storage space.
Authoritative Links:
BigQuery views: Explanation of how BigQuery views function.
BigQuery authorized views: Explanation of how to authorize a view to access a different dataset.
BigQuery IAM roles: Overview of IAM roles and permissions for BigQuery.
Principle of Least Privilege: Google Cloud's approach to least privilege.

question: Your operations team currently stores 10 TB of data in an object storage service from a third-party provider. They want to move this data to a Cloud Storage bucket as quickly as possible, following Google-recommended practices. They want to minimize the cost of this data migration. Which approach should they use?
options: Use the gsutil mv command to move the data.
Use the Storage Transfer Service to move the data.
Download the data to a Transfer Appliance, and ship it to Google.
Download the data to the on-premises data center, and upload it to the Cloud Storage bucket.
explanation:
The optimal approach is to use Storage Transfer Service (STS). Here's why: STS is a fully managed, serverless service designed specifically for large-scale data transfers into and out of Google Cloud Storage. Unlike gsutil mv, which is primarily intended for file management within Cloud Storage, STS is optimized for transferring data from various sources, including third-party object storage. STS offers features like automated retry logic, scheduling, and bandwidth throttling, ensuring a reliable and efficient migration. Downloading to a Transfer Appliance (option C) or an on-premises data center (option D) adds unnecessary steps, increasing complexity, time, and cost. While a Transfer Appliance is suitable for extremely large datasets or situations with limited network connectivity, 10TB doesn't warrant its use, introducing delays and expenses.
Downloading to an on-premises location (option D) is also inefficient as it requires intermediate storage and upload processes. Given the scale of 10TB and the requirement for minimizing cost, STS provides a cost- effective and streamlined approach. It leverages Google’s network infrastructure to deliver high-performance transfers, and users only pay for the actual data transferred. It also allows for efficient management of the entire process via its web console or API. STS minimizes operational overhead and offers a more secure path for migrating data compared to manual approaches.
Authoritative Link:Storage Transfer Service Overview

question:
You have a Compute Engine managed instance group that adds and removes Compute Engine instances from the group in response to the load on your application. The instances have a shutdown script that removes REDIS database entries associated with the instance. You see that many database entries have not been removed, and you suspect that the shutdown script is the problem. You need to ensure that the commands in the shutdown script are run reliably every time an instance is shut down. You create a Cloud Function to remove the database entries.
What should you do next?
options: Modify the shutdown script to wait for 30 seconds before triggering the Cloud Function.
Do not use the Cloud Function. Modify the shutdown script to restart if it has not completed in 30 seconds.
Set up a Cloud Monitoring sink that triggers the Cloud Function after an instance removal log message arrives in Cloud Logging.
Modify the shutdown script to wait for 30 seconds and then publish a message to a Pub/Sub queue.
explanation:
Here's a detailed justification for why option C is the correct approach, along with explanations of why the others are less suitable:
Option C: Set up a Cloud Monitoring sink that triggers the Cloud Function after an instance removal log message arrives in Cloud Logging.
This is the most reliable solution for ensuring cleanup tasks happen during instance termination in a managed instance group. Here's why:
Event-Driven Architecture: Cloud Logging provides near real-time log data. By creating a sink that filters for instance removal events, we can trigger the Cloud Function after the instance is officially marked for deletion, not during its potentially unreliable shutdown process. This is an example of an event-driven architecture.
Reliability: Shutdown scripts, as indicated in the problem, can be unreliable as they are executed by the instance during its shutdown phase. If the instance terminates abruptly (or the script takes too long), it might not complete execution. Using Cloud Logging and a Cloud Function removes the dependency on the instance's health and lifecycle for running the cleanup.
Decoupling: The Cloud Function performs the cleanup outside of the instance, further decoupling the application logic from the instance lifecycle management, leading to a more robust architecture.
Scalability: This approach scales well with a managed instance group. No matter how many instances are being removed or scaled down, the Cloud Function handles the cleanup process automatically when a log message indicates it.
Loose Coupling: The instance does not need to know anything about the cleanup function. This helps to limit the complexity of the instance image.
Best Practices: Google Cloud best practices encourage using an event-driven design for lifecycle events like instance termination.
Why other options are less suitable:
Option A: Modify the shutdown script to wait for 30 seconds before triggering the Cloud Function. This is problematic because relying on the shutdown script to trigger anything, even after waiting, is still not reliable.
The instance might terminate before the script has a chance to complete. Also, 30 seconds might not be enough in some cases.
Option B: Do not use the Cloud Function. Modify the shutdown script to restart if it has not completed in 30 seconds. Restarting the shutdown script creates a loop that does not actually remove the database entries, plus it introduces more complexity to the shutdown script, and is still unreliable. It does not guarantee completion, and the instance could still be terminated before the script completes. Moreover, it is not the intended use of the shutdown script.
Option D: Modify the shutdown script to wait for 30 seconds and then publish a message to a Pub/Sub queue. While slightly better than options A and B (as it's asynchronous), it still relies on the shutdown script completing reliably, making it a less robust solution than triggering based on a log event. There's no guarantee the message will be published before instance termination. Furthermore, introducing Pub/Sub unnecessarily increases the complexity of the solution.
Authoritative Links:
Cloud Logging Sinks: https://cloud.google.com/logging/docs/export/configure_export_v2 Cloud Functions: https://cloud.google.com/functions/docs Managed Instance Groups: https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/instance-groups Shutdown Scripts: https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/startupscript#shutdownscript In summary, using a Cloud Logging sink to trigger a Cloud Function provides the most reliable, scalable, and decoupled approach for handling cleanup tasks during instance termination in a managed instance group.

question: You are managing several projects on Google Cloud and need to interact on a daily basis with BigQuery, Bigtable, and Kubernetes Engine using the gcloud CL tool. You are travelling a lot and work on different workstations during the week. You want to avoid having to manage the gcloud CLI manually. What should you do?
options: Use Google Cloud Shell in the Google Cloud Console to interact with Google Cloud.
Create a Compute Engine instance and install gcloud on the instance. Connect to this instance via SSH to always use the same gcloud installation when interacting with Google Cloud.
Install gcloud on all of your workstations. Run the command gcloud components auto-update on each workstation
Use a package manager to install gcloud on your workstations instead of installing it manually.
explanation:
The most effective solution for managing gcloud CLI access across multiple workstations without manual configuration is to leverage Google Cloud Shell (option A). Google Cloud Shell is a browser-based, pre- configured, and temporary shell environment readily accessible within the Google Cloud Console. This eliminates the need to install and manage gcloud and its associated dependencies on individual workstations.
It provides a consistent and readily available environment regardless of the user's location or machine. Since it's web-based, the user can access it securely from any internet-connected device with a browser, ensuring continuity of workflow. Furthermore, the Cloud Shell instance is pre-authenticated with the user's Google Cloud credentials, allowing direct access to managed resources without further configuration. Options B, C, and D introduce unnecessary administrative overhead. Option B requires managing a Compute Engine instance and establishing SSH connections, adding complexity and operational burden. Options C and D, while providing local access, require repeated manual installation and updates on multiple machines, which is exactly what the scenario wants to avoid. Cloud Shell's serverless, ephemeral nature makes it inherently more suitable for managing interactions from varying locations. It also provides 5GB of persistent disk storage for scripts and configuration files. Lastly, it simplifies access to cloud resources without exposing gcloud credentials directly on potentially insecure machines.
Relevant Links:
Google Cloud Shell Documentation: https://cloud.google.com/shell/docs Google Cloud CLI Documentation: https://cloud.google.com/sdk/docs/

question: Your company recently acquired a company that has infrastructure in Google Cloud. Each company has its own Google Cloud organization. Each company is using a Shared Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) to provide network connectivity for its applications. Some of the subnets used by both companies overlap. In order for both businesses to integrate, the applications need to have private network connectivity. These applications are not on overlapping subnets. You want to provide connectivity with minimal re-engineering. What should you do?
options: Set up VPC peering and peer each Shared VPC together.
Migrate the projects from the acquired company into your company's Google Cloud organization. Re-launch the instances in your companies Shared VPC.
Set up a Cloud VPN gateway in each Shared VPC and peer Cloud VPNs.
Configure SSH port forwarding on each application to provide connectivity between applications in the different Shared VPCs.
explanation:
The correct answer is C, establishing Cloud VPN gateways in each Shared VPC and peering them. This approach effectively addresses the network connectivity challenge with minimal disruption. Option A, using VPC peering directly, fails because VPC peering cannot be established between networks with overlapping subnets. Option B, migrating projects and relaunching instances, involves significant re-engineering and downtime, violating the requirement for minimal change. Option D, relying on SSH port forwarding, is unsuitable for production environments due to its complexity, security risks, and performance limitations; it's not a scalable or reliable solution for application-to-application communication. Cloud VPN, on the other hand, allows secure IPsec tunnels to be established between the Shared VPCs, enabling private communication even with overlapping subnets because the traffic is encapsulated and routed via the VPN gateways. This solution also adheres to the principle of least privilege, as it focuses on the network layer rather than modifying application configurations. Moreover, it provides a stable and secure channel for data exchange, which is crucial for integrating two businesses. This method allows organizations to avoid IP conflict and complexity, and maintain a secure private connection.
Authoritative Links:
Google Cloud Documentation - Cloud VPN: https://cloud.google.com/vpn/docs Google Cloud Documentation - Shared VPC: https://cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/shared-vpc Google Cloud Documentation - VPC Peering: https://cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/vpc-peering

question: You are managing several internal applications that are deployed on Compute Engine. Business users inform you that an application has become very slow over the past few days. You want to find the underlying cause in order to solve the problem. What should you do first?
options: Inspect the logs and metrics from the instances in Cloud Logging and Cloud Monitoring.
Change the Compute Engine Instances behind the application to a machine type with more CPU and memory.
Restore a backup of the application database from a time before the application became slow.
Deploy the applications on a managed instance group with autoscaling enabled. Add a load balancer in front of the managed instance group, and have the users connect to the IP of the load balancer.
explanation:
The most appropriate first step when troubleshooting a slow application on Compute Engine is to inspect the logs and metrics from the instances in Cloud Logging and Cloud Monitoring (A). This proactive approach allows for a data-driven diagnosis before making potentially unnecessary and costly changes. Cloud Monitoring provides crucial performance metrics, including CPU utilization, memory usage, disk I/O, and network traffic, which can pinpoint resource bottlenecks. Simultaneously, Cloud Logging gathers application and system logs, offering insights into errors, exceptions, or unusual behaviors that may contribute to the slowdown. Analyzing both metrics and logs provides a holistic view, helping to identify the root cause – whether it's resource contention, inefficient code, database issues, or networking problems. Attempting solutions like increasing resources (B), restoring backups (C), or implementing autoscaling and load balancing (D) without this initial diagnosis is premature and may not address the underlying issue, potentially resulting in wasted resources and time. A systematic approach leveraging monitoring and logging is fundamental for effective troubleshooting and is a best practice in cloud environments.
Further Research:
Cloud Monitoring Documentation: https://cloud.google.com/monitoring/docs Cloud Logging Documentation: https://cloud.google.com/logging/docs Google Cloud Best Practices for Performance Optimization:

question: Your company has an application running as a Deployment in a Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) cluster. When releasing new versions of the application via a rolling deployment, the team has been causing outages. The root cause of the outages is misconfigurations with parameters that are only used in production. You want to put preventive measures for this in the platform to prevent outages. What should you do?
options: Configure liveness and readiness probes in the Pod specification.
Configure health checks on the managed instance group.
Create a Scheduled Task to check whether the application is available.
Configure an uptime alert in Cloud Monitoring.
explanation:
The correct answer is A. Configure liveness and readiness probes in the Pod specification.
Here's the justification:
Liveness and readiness probes are crucial for managing application health within a Kubernetes environment like GKE. Liveness probes detect when an application has become unhealthy and needs to be restarted.
Readiness probes signal when an application is ready to accept traffic. By configuring these probes in the Pod specification, Kubernetes can automatically detect and remediate issues caused by misconfigurations during rolling deployments. When a new pod with misconfigurations starts, the liveness probe might fail, causing the pod to be restarted, or the readiness probe might fail, preventing traffic from reaching the pod. This prevents the new faulty pod from impacting the overall application health. These probes directly address the issue of outages caused by misconfigured parameters that appear only in production by monitoring the application's internal state. While options B, C, and D offer valuable monitoring and alerting capabilities, they don't actively prevent unhealthy pods from impacting the deployment during rolling updates like liveness and readiness probes do. For example, an uptime alert (D) would only notify about an issue after it has occurred, not prevent it in the first place.
Authoritative links:
Kubernetes Liveness Probes: https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/configure-pod-container/configure-liveness- readiness-startup-probes/ Kubernetes Readiness Probes: https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/configure-pod-container/configure-liveness- readiness-startup-probes/ Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) Deployments: https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes- engine/docs/concepts/deployments

question: Your company uses Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) as a platform for all workloads. Your company has a single large GKE cluster that contains batch, stateful, and stateless workloads. The GKE cluster is configured with a single node pool with 200 nodes. Your company needs to reduce the cost of this cluster but does not want to compromise availability. What should you do?
options: Create a second GKE cluster for the batch workloads only. Allocate the 200 original nodes across both clusters.
Configure CPU and memory limits on the namespaces in the cluster. Configure all Pods to have a CPU and memory limits.
Configure a HorizontalPodAutoscaler for all stateless workloads and for all compatible stateful workloads. Configure the cluster to use node auto scaling.
Change the node pool to use preemptible VMs.
explanation:
Option C is the most suitable solution because it addresses both cost reduction and availability without requiring significant architectural changes. Here's why:
Horizontal Pod Autoscaling (HPA): HPA dynamically adjusts the number of pod replicas based on observed CPU utilization or other custom metrics. This ensures that resources are only consumed when needed, leading to cost savings during periods of low demand. By scaling down stateless and compatible stateful workloads during off-peak times, resource waste is minimized.
Node Autoscaling: Node autoscaling complements HPA by adding or removing nodes in the cluster based on resource requests. If scaled pods require more resources than the current nodes can provide, the cluster will automatically add new nodes, ensuring availability. Conversely, when demand decreases, nodes can be removed to save costs. This dynamic adjustment keeps resources aligned with the actual requirements of the cluster.
Resource Optimization: The combination of HPA and node autoscaling optimizes resource consumption by scaling up or down as needed. This avoids over-provisioning that is common when a fixed number of nodes is always running, thus reducing costs.
Availability: By using autoscaling, the cluster is more resilient. During peak usage, the system can expand capacity to meet demand. If nodes fail, node autoscaling can quickly replace them, reducing service disruption.
No Architectural Overhaul: Option A involves creating a second cluster, which requires significant architectural changes and management overhead. Option B limits resources, which can negatively affect availability during peak times if limits are too low. Option D makes use of preemptible VMs, which can be reclaimed by GCP and are not suitable for workloads needing high availability.
Authoritative Links:
Kubernetes Horizontal Pod Autoscaling: https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/run-application/horizontal-pod- autoscale/ GKE Node Autoscaling: https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/node-autoscaling Best Practices for Running Cost-Effective Applications on Google Kubernetes Engine:
applications-on-gke

question: Your company has a Google Cloud project that uses BigQuery for data warehousing on a pay-per-use basis. You want to monitor queries in real time to discover the most costly queries and which users spend the most. What should you do?
options: 1. In the BigQuery dataset that contains all the tables to be queried, add a label for each user that can launch a query. 2. Open the Billing page of the project. 3. Select Reports. 4. Select BigQuery as the product and filter by the user you want to check.
1. Create a Cloud Logging sink to export BigQuery data access logs to BigQuery. 2. Perform a BigQuery query on the generated table to extract the information you need.
1. Create a Cloud Logging sink to export BigQuery data access logs to Cloud Storage. 2. Develop a Dataflow pipeline to compute the cost of queries split by users.
1. Activate billing export into BigQuery. 2. Perform a BigQuery query on the billing table to extract the information you need.
explanation:
Option B is the correct approach because it leverages the power of BigQuery's audit logs and its querying capabilities to provide real-time insights into query costs and user activity.
Here's a breakdown:
1. Cloud Logging Sink: BigQuery data access logs record detailed information about queries, including the user who executed them, the query's execution time, and the resources consumed. By creating a Cloud Logging sink that exports these logs to another BigQuery dataset (as suggested in option B), we can easily query this raw data. https://cloud.google.com/logging/docs/export 2. BigQuery Querying: Once the logs are in BigQuery, we can use standard SQL queries to analyze the data. This allows us to perform aggregations, filtering, and groupings to discover which queries are most expensive and which users spend the most on queries. We can calculate the cost based on the processing time and other resources consumed by each query, and group that data by the user that initiated the query. https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/querying-data Option A is incorrect because while labels can be used, they would not automatically track the cost of queries on a per-user basis; labels are useful for resource grouping and management. Filtering by user in the billing page is useful for cost analysis, but it doesn't provide the detailed query-level visibility the question is asking for.
Option C is incorrect because using Dataflow for cost analysis when the raw data is already within BigQuery is less efficient and adds unnecessary complexity. While Dataflow is useful, it's an overkill for the requirements.
Also Dataflow will require additional costs and complexity.
Option D is incorrect because while billing export to BigQuery provides a breakdown of overall costs by service and project, it doesn’t provide detailed information on individual BigQuery queries and the users that generated them. It will not allow to determine which exact query is more costly and which user spends more on the queries.
Therefore, option B offers the most direct and efficient way to gain the required insights by leveraging logging data and BigQuery's native analytical capabilities. It allows near real-time insights into query costs, split by user, by using the generated logs from the Cloud Logging sink exported into BigQuery.

question: Your company and one of its partners each have a Google Cloud project in separate organizations. Your company's project (prj-a) runs in Virtual Private Cloud (vpc-a). The partner's project (prj-b) runs in vpc-b. There are two instances running on vpc-a and one instance running on vpc-b. Subnets defined in both VPCs are not overlapping. You need to ensure that all instances communicate with each other via internal IPs, minimizing latency and maximizing throughput. What should you do?
options: Set up a network peering between vpc-a and vpc-b.
Set up a VPN between vpc-a and vpc-b using Cloud VPN.

Configure IAP TCP forwarding on the instance in vpc-b, and then launch the following gcloud command from one of the instances in vpc-a gcloud: gcloud compute start-iap-tunnel INSTANCE_NAME_IN_VPC_8 22 \ -- local-host-port=localhost:22

1. Create an additional instance in vpc-a. 2. Create an additional instance in vpc-b. 3. Install OpenVPN in newly created instances. 4. Configure a VPN tunnel between vpc-a and vpc-b with the help of OpenVPN.
explanation:
The correct answer is A. Set up a network peering between vpc-a and vpc-b.
Here's why:
Network peering allows direct, private communication between Virtual Private Clouds (VPCs) in Google Cloud.
This establishes a secure, low-latency connection using Google's internal network. Since the subnets are non- overlapping, peering is a feasible and optimal solution. This method avoids the overhead and complexities associated with other options, like VPNs.
By peering vpc-a and vpc-b, instances in both projects can communicate using their internal IP addresses. This satisfies the requirements of minimizing latency and maximizing throughput, as traffic doesn't need to traverse the public internet. There are no extra costs associated with egress traffic to the public internet.
Peering offers a scalable approach for inter-VPC communication.
Option B, using Cloud VPN, introduces the overhead of encryption and decryption, increasing latency and potentially reducing throughput compared to peering. While VPNs are useful for connecting to on-premise networks, peering is better suited for VPC-to-VPC communication within Google Cloud.
Option C, employing IAP TCP forwarding, is designed to provide secure access to instances via the public internet and is unsuitable for direct internal IP communication between VPCs. It's also more complex and less efficient than peering for this use case.
Option D, using OpenVPN, involves manual setup, management overhead, and the potential for configuration errors. Furthermore, relying on two new instances just for networking adds unnecessary complexity, and OpenVPN itself is a resource-intensive option compared to the native peering option.
In summary, VPC peering is the most straightforward, efficient, and scalable solution to enable low-latency, high-throughput communication between instances residing in separate Google Cloud VPCs with non- overlapping subnets.
Authoritative Links:
VPC Network Peering Overview: https://cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/vpc-peering Comparing Peering with VPNs: https://cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/choose-vpc-peering-vpn IAP TCP Forwarding: https://cloud.google.com/iap/docs/tcp-forwarding-howto

question:
You want to store critical business information in Cloud Storage buckets. The information is regularly changed, but previous versions need to be referenced on a regular basis. You want to ensure that there is a record of all changes to any information in these buckets. You want to ensure that accidental edits or deletions can be easily rolled back.
Which feature should you enable?
options: Bucket Lock
Object Versioning
Object change notification
Object Lifecycle Management
explanation:
The correct answer is B. Object Versioning. Object Versioning in Google Cloud Storage is specifically designed to address the need to retain previous versions of objects, making it ideal for scenarios where data is regularly changed and rollbacks are necessary. When enabled, every time an object is overwritten or deleted, Cloud Storage creates a new version of the object, preserving the previous state. This ensures a historical record of all changes. Bucket Lock, while important for compliance, prevents modifications once enabled and isn't for regular rollbacks. Object change notification alerts you to changes but doesn't store previous versions. Object Lifecycle Management automates object deletion or storage class changes and isn't meant for version history. Therefore, Object Versioning perfectly fulfills the requirements of tracking all changes, referencing past states, and easily rolling back accidental edits or deletions.
Authoritative links for further research:
Google Cloud Storage Object Versioning documentation: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/object- versioning Overview of Cloud Storage Features: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/overview

question:
You have a Compute Engine application that you want to autoscale when total memory usage exceeds 80%. You have installed the Cloud Monitoring agent and configured the autoscaling policy as follows:
✑ Metric identifier: agent.googleapis.com/memory/percent_used ✑ Filter: metric.label.state = 'used' ✑ Target utilization level: 80 ✑ Target type: GAUGE You observe that the application does not scale under high load. You want to resolve this. What should you do?
options: Change the Target type to DELTA_PER_MINUTE.
Change the Metric identifier to agent.googleapis.com/memory/bytes_used.
Change the filter to metric.label.state = 'used' AND metric.label.state = 'buffered' AND metric.label.state = 'cached' AND metric.label.state = 'slab'.
Change the filter to metric.label.state = 'free' and the Target utilization to 20.
explanation:
The issue lies in the GAUGE target type used with the percent_used memory metric. GAUGE targets a specific absolute value at a given point in time. Memory usage, as a percentage, can fluctuate rapidly. Autoscaling needs to react to the change in the metric over time to effectively trigger scaling. Option A, changing the target type to DELTA_PER_MINUTE, is the correct solution. DELTA_PER_MINUTE calculates the rate of change of the metric per minute, allowing autoscaling to respond to trends in memory usage, rather than instantaneous peaks or valleys. This rate of change provides a more stable indicator for scaling decisions. The original GAUGE target could be misleading, as a brief spike in usage might trigger a scale-up, but if the usage drops immediately after, it was an inappropriate scaling response. DELTA_PER_MINUTE averages changes over time and provides a better picture of a sustained memory pressure.
Options B, C, and D are incorrect. Option B suggests changing the metric to bytes_used, which would still be a GAUGE value and not solve the problem of fluctuating absolute values. Option C proposes a very specific and likely unnecessary filter that adds cached/buffered/slab memory, whereas focusing on the overall used percentage is sufficient for autoscaling. Finally, option D’s proposal of free memory and 20% target utilization is counterintuitive and incorrect because the goal is to scale based on used memory.
Supporting Links:
Autoscaling based on metrics: https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/autoscaling/autoscaling-metrics (Specifically, focus on target types and usage for metrics).
Cloud Monitoring Metrics: https://cloud.google.com/monitoring/api/metrics_agent (Review the agent.googleapis.com/memory metrics and their types).
Target types in Autoscaling: https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/autoscaling/autoscaling- metrics#target_utilization (This clearly explains different target types including GAUGE and DELTA_PER_MINUTE.)

question:
You are deploying an application to Google Cloud. The application is part of a system. The application in Google Cloud must communicate over a private network with applications in a non-Google Cloud environment. The expected average throughput is 200 kbps. The business requires:
✑ as close to 100% system availability as possible ✑ cost optimization You need to design the connectivity between the locations to meet the business requirements. What should you provision?
options: An HA Cloud VPN gateway connected with two tunnels to an on-premises VPN gateway
Two Classic Cloud VPN gateways connected to two on-premises VPN gateways Configure each Classic Cloud VPN gateway to have two tunnels, each connected to different on-premises VPN gateways
Two HA Cloud VPN gateways connected to two on-premises VPN gateways Configure each HA Cloud VPN gateway to have two tunnels, each connected to different on-premises VPN gateways
A single Cloud VPN gateway connected to an on-premises VPN gateway
explanation:
The correct answer is A: An HA Cloud VPN gateway connected with two tunnels to an on-premises VPN gateway. This solution optimally balances high availability and cost for the given requirements.
Justification:
High Availability: HA Cloud VPN utilizes two interfaces to create redundant VPN tunnels connecting to the on-premise VPN gateway. If one tunnel or interface fails, traffic automatically shifts to the other, ensuring continuous connectivity and maximizing system availability, which is a core business requirement.
Cost Optimization: While multiple gateways might seem like a robust solution, HA Cloud VPN offers redundancy within a single, managed service, optimizing for cost compared to deploying multiple independent gateways and their associated infrastructure. Using two tunnels per HA VPN gateway provides the necessary redundancy without incurring the expense of multiple gateways.
Throughput Requirement: The 200 kbps throughput is quite low. HA Cloud VPN tunnels can easily handle this level of traffic and are typically sized for much higher capacity, making it a suitable fit.
Avoidance of Redundant Gateways: Option B and C propose multiple gateways which increase complexity and cost without significant added value for the given throughput and availability needs. Option D presents a single point of failure. HA VPN inherently offers the needed redundancy.
Simplicity: Using a single HA VPN setup, as proposed in option A, simplifies the configuration and management compared to options B and C which would require managing multiple gateways.
Relevant Cloud Computing Concepts:
High Availability (HA): Designing systems to minimize downtime and ensure continuous operation, often through redundancy.
Virtual Private Network (VPN): A secure encrypted tunnel that creates a private connection across a public network.
Cloud VPN: Google Cloud's service to create VPN connections to other networks using IPsec VPN tunnels.
Active-Active Redundancy: Both tunnels actively route traffic, providing more bandwidth and redundancy.
Cost-Optimization: Choosing the most efficient resource usage to minimize expenditure.
Authoritative Links:
Cloud VPN Overview: https://cloud.google.com/vpn/docs/concepts/overview HA VPN: https://cloud.google.com/network-connectivity/docs/vpn/concepts/ha-vpn Choosing a Cloud VPN Topology: https://cloud.google.com/network-connectivity/docs/vpn/how-to/choosing- a-topology In conclusion, utilizing a single HA Cloud VPN gateway with dual tunnels efficiently addresses the requirement for both high availability and cost optimization for this specific scenario. The low bandwidth needs do not justify a more complex and costly multi-gateway approach.

question:
Your company has an application running on App Engine that allows users to upload music files and share them with other people. You want to allow users to upload files directly into Cloud Storage from their browser session.
The payload should not be passed through the backend. What should you do?
options: 1. Set a CORS configuration in the target Cloud Storage bucket where the base URL of the App Engine application is an allowed origin. 2. Use the Cloud Storage Signed URL feature to generate a POST URL.

1. Set a CORS configuration in the target Cloud Storage bucket where the base URL of the App Engine application is an allowed origin. 2. Assign the Cloud Storage WRITER role to users who upload files.
1. Use the Cloud Storage Signed URL feature to generate a POST URL. 2. Use App Engine default credentials to sign requests against Cloud Storage.
1. Assign the Cloud Storage WRITER role to users who upload files. 2. Use App Engine default credentials to sign requests against Cloud Storage.
explanation:
Here's a detailed justification for why option A is the correct solution, along with explanations of why the other options are incorrect:
Justification for Option A:
Option A correctly addresses the requirement of direct browser uploads to Cloud Storage, bypassing the App Engine backend. This is achieved through two key steps:
1. CORS Configuration: Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS) is crucial for web applications to interact with resources from different origins (domains). Since the App Engine application is hosted on one domain and the Cloud Storage bucket on another, a CORS configuration on the bucket is required. This configuration specifies which origins (in this case, the base URL of the App Engine application) are permitted to make requests to the bucket. Without proper CORS, browser security mechanisms will block direct uploads. This is an important security consideration, allowing resources from different origins to interact with each other without having unrestricted access.
2. Signed URL: A Signed URL is a time-limited URL that grants specific permissions to access a Cloud Storage resource. By generating a POST-type Signed URL, the application can provide a user’s browser with the necessary credentials to directly upload a file to the designated location in Cloud Storage. This avoids the need for the server to receive the file initially and then relay it to Cloud Storage, reducing server load and latency.
Therefore, Option A correctly uses both CORS and Signed URLs to achieve direct browser uploads without the backend's involvement.
Why other Options are Incorrect:
Option B: While setting a CORS configuration is correct, assigning the Cloud Storage WRITER role directly to users is a significant security risk. Users shouldn't have direct permanent write access to the bucket. This would violate the principle of least privilege, providing excessive access and leading to potential security vulnerabilities.
Option C: While using Signed URLs is correct for client-side uploads, App Engine default credentials are not intended for granting client access. They should be used only to access services from within your App Engine application for server-to-server communication. Using them directly exposes sensitive information and presents a security risk.
Option D: This option combines the major issues of B and C, by directly granting WRITER role and improperly using default credentials, further amplifying the security vulnerabilities.
Authoritative Links for Further Research:
Cloud Storage CORS: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/configuring-cors Cloud Storage Signed URLs: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/access-control/signed-urls Principle of Least Privilege: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Principle_of_least_privilege In conclusion, option A is the only solution that correctly and securely enables direct browser uploads to Cloud Storage, adhering to best practices and avoiding potential security risks.

question:
You are configuring the cloud network architecture for a newly created project in Google Cloud that will host applications in Compute Engine. Compute Engine virtual machine instances will be created in two different subnets (sub-a and sub-b) within a single region:
•Instances in sub-a will have public IP addresses.
•Instances in sub-b will have only private IP addresses.
To download updated packages, instances must connect to a public repository outside the boundaries of Google Cloud. You need to allow sub-b to access the external repository. What should you do?
options: Enable Private Google Access on sub-b.
Configure Cloud NAT and select sub-b in the NAT mapping section.
Configure a bastion host instance in sub-a to connect to instances in sub-b.
Enable Identity-Aware Proxy for TCP forwarding for instances in sub-b.
explanation:
Here's a detailed justification for why option B, "Configure Cloud NAT and select sub-b in the NAT mapping section," is the correct solution:
The core requirement is to allow instances in sub-b, which have private IP addresses, to access external resources (a public repository) without directly exposing them to the internet. Cloud NAT (Network Address Translation) is designed precisely for this purpose. It allows instances within a private network to initiate outbound connections to the internet by translating their private IP addresses to a public IP address belonging to the Cloud NAT gateway. This ensures that outbound traffic appears to originate from a public IP address instead of the internal ones.
Option A, enabling Private Google Access, allows instances with private IP addresses to access Google Cloud APIs and services, but it doesn't enable access to arbitrary external endpoints. Thus, it would not solve the problem of accessing a public repository outside Google Cloud. Option C, using a bastion host, adds unnecessary complexity. While a bastion host can provide connectivity, it requires managing an additional instance and setting up forwarding rules. Cloud NAT is a more direct and scalable solution. Option D, Identity- Aware Proxy, focuses on controlling access to applications and resources; it’s not relevant for basic outbound internet connectivity.
By configuring Cloud NAT and selecting sub-b in the NAT mapping, the instances in sub-b can send traffic through the NAT gateway. The gateway will translate the source IP addresses of these instances to a public IP address, allowing access to the external repository. Return traffic is directed back to the private instance using NAT. This way, instances in sub-b can access the internet without needing their own public IPs, adhering to best practices for security and network management. Cloud NAT scales automatically based on demand making it a robust solution.
Authoritative Links:
Cloud NAT Overview: https://cloud.google.com/nat/docs/overview Configuring Cloud NAT: https://cloud.google.com/nat/docs/using-nat Private Google Access: https://cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/private-access-options

question: Your company is planning to migrate their Windows Server 2022 from their on-premises data center to Google Cloud. You need to bring the licenses that are currently in use in on-premises virtual machines into the target cloud environment. What should you do?
options: 1. Create an image of the on-premises virtual machines and upload into Cloud Storage. 2. Import the image as a virtual disk on Compute Engine.
1. Create standard instances on Compute Engine. 2. Select as the OS the same Microsoft Windows version that is currently in use in the on-premises environment.

1. Create an image of the on-premises virtual machine. 2. Import the image as a virtual disk on Compute Engine. 3. Create a standard instance on Compute Engine, selecting as the OS the same Microsoft Windows version that is currently in use in the on-premises environment. 4. Attach a data disk that includes data that matches the created image.

1. Create an image of the on-premises virtual machines. 2. Import the image as a virtual disk on Compute Engine using --os=windows-2022-dc-v. 3. Create a sole-tenancy instance on Compute Engine that uses the imported disk as a boot disk.
explanation:
The correct answer is D because it outlines the proper procedure for migrating Windows Server 2022 licenses to Google Cloud using the bring-your-own-license (BYOL) model via sole-tenancy nodes. Option D starts by creating an image of the on-premises virtual machine, capturing its operating system, applications, and configurations. This image is then imported into Compute Engine, specifically specifying the operating system as windows-2022-dc-v which indicates a BYOL license. The key step is then creating a sole-tenancy instance.
Sole-tenancy nodes allow customers to bring their existing Windows Server licenses, aligning with the requirement of utilizing current on-premises licenses. BYOL is the recommended and cost-effective approach in such scenario. Option A is incorrect as importing the image without specifying the OS does not allow proper license mapping. Option B is incorrect because creating standard Compute Engine instances uses Google's licenses, not your own, failing to fulfill the BYOL requirement. Option C is also incorrect. Creating a separate standard instance and then attaching a data disk does not correctly handle Windows licenses. Instead, the OS disk needs to be from the image you provide that has the existing windows setup, and needs to be run in a sole-tenancy node for license purposes. Sole tenancy and BYOL are critical for compliance and cost optimization in this scenario.
Supporting Links:
Google Cloud Sole-Tenancy Nodes: https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/nodes/sole-tenant-nodes Bring Your Own License (BYOL): https://cloud.google.com/licensing Importing Virtual Disks: https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/import/import-virtual-disks

question:
You are deploying an application to Google Cloud. The application is part of a system. The application in Google Cloud must communicate over a private network with applications in a non-Google Cloud environment. The expected average throughput is 200 kbps. The business requires:
•99.99% system availability •cost optimization You need to design the connectivity between the locations to meet the business requirements. What should you provision?
options: An HA Cloud VPN gateway connected with two tunnels to an on-premises VPN gateway.
A Classic Cloud VPN gateway connected with two tunnels to an on-premises VPN gateway.
Two HA Cloud VPN gateways connected to two on-premises VPN gateways. Configure each HA Cloud VPN gateway to have two tunnels, each connected to different on-premises VPN gateways.
A Classic Cloud VPN gateway connected with one tunnel to an on-premises VPN gateway.
explanation: Option A, an HA Cloud VPN gateway with two tunnels to an on-premises VPN gateway, is the correct choice because it provides the required 99.99% availability and cost optimization for the described scenario. High Availability (HA) Cloud VPN gateways inherently provide redundancy by having two interfaces, meaning that the connection remains active even if one interface fails. The use of two tunnels enhances this redundancy, ensuring that if one tunnel experiences issues, the other can maintain the connection. A single HA gateway is more cost-effective than using two full HA gateways as in Option C, while still meeting the high availability requirement. Option B, using a Classic Cloud VPN, doesn’t meet the 99.99% availability target as classic VPN gateways lack inherent redundancy. Option D, while cost-effective, sacrifices availability with a single point of failure. Option C is overkill for a relatively low 200 kbps throughput and doesn't contribute substantially to increased performance. It would also unnecessarily increase costs. The HA VPN solution with two tunnels provides a balance of reliability and cost efficiency, leveraging the HA feature for failover protection of the primary connection. This solution ensures a stable and resilient connection for the application’s private network communication needs while remaining within budget constraints. For further information, refer to Google's documentation on Cloud VPN: https://cloud.google.com/vpn/docs/concepts/overview, specifically on HA VPN configuration.

question: Your company wants to migrate their 10-TB on-premises database export into Cloud Storage. You want to minimize the time it takes to complete this activity and the overall cost. The bandwidth between the on-premises environment and Google Cloud is 1 Gbps. You want to follow Google-recommended practices. What should you do?
options: Develop a Dataflow job to read data directly from the database and write it into Cloud Storage.
Use the Data Transfer appliance to perform an offline migration.
Use a commercial partner ETL solution to extract the data from the on-premises database and upload it into Cloud Storage.
Upload the data with gcloud storage cp.
explanation:
The correct answer is D. Upload the data with gcloud storage cp. Here's why:
While options A, B, and C have their place, they are not the most efficient or cost-effective solutions for a straightforward 10TB database export transfer, given the 1 Gbps bandwidth constraint and the goal of minimizing time and cost. Option A, using Dataflow, involves complex processing and is unnecessary for a simple data transfer. Option B, the Data Transfer appliance, is designed for very large datasets exceeding network bandwidth limitations and involves additional logistics, thus is an overkill for a 10TB database export with 1 Gbps connection. Option C, relying on a commercial ETL solution, introduces costs and complexity without adding direct value to a simple file transfer.
gcloud storage cp is a command-line tool that leverages the available network bandwidth effectively for direct uploads to Cloud Storage. It's highly optimized, can handle large files, and is the most straightforward method for this scenario. The tool's ability to split large files into smaller chunks for parallel transfer improves throughput. It avoids the overhead and complexities associated with more elaborate solutions. The question emphasizes both speed and cost reduction, and gcloud cp offers the best balance of both for transferring the database export files directly. Cloud Storage is designed to receive large amounts of data quickly using command line tools like gcloud storage cp.
Supporting Links:
Google Cloud Storage Documentation on gcloud storage cp:
Google Cloud Data Transfer Options: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/data-transfer In summary, gcloud storage cp provides a simple, direct, cost-effective and fast way to leverage the provided 1Gbps connection to transfer the database export to Cloud Storage.

question: You are working at a financial institution that stores mortgage loan approval documents on Cloud Storage. Any change to these approval documents must be uploaded as a separate approval file. You need to ensure that these documents cannot be deleted or overwritten for the next 5 years. What should you do?
options: Create a retention policy on the bucket for the duration of 5 years. Create a lock on the retention policy.
Create a retention policy organizational constraint constraints/storage.retentionPolicySeconds at the organization level. Set the duration to 5 years.
Use a customer-managed key for the encryption of the bucket. Rotate the key after 5 years.
Create a retention policy organizational constraint constraints/storage.retentionPolicySeconds at the project level. Set the duration to 5 years.
explanation:
Option A, creating a retention policy on the bucket for 5 years and then locking it, is the correct approach because it directly addresses the requirement of preventing deletion or overwriting of objects within a Cloud Storage bucket for a specified duration. Retention policies in Cloud Storage are designed for data immutability and compliance. When a retention policy is locked, it becomes permanent, and neither the policy nor the objects it covers can be deleted or modified before the policy's duration expires. This ensures adherence to the 5-year retention requirement, aligning with regulatory obligations common in financial institutions dealing with sensitive documents. Option B, applying a retention policy as an organizational constraint, sets a minimum duration for all buckets within the organization. While this prevents deletion, it doesn't guarantee a specific 5-year immutability window for this bucket, and allows a longer duration if applied at a bucket level. Option C, using a customer-managed key (CMK) for encryption, addresses encryption at rest, but does not prevent deletion or overwriting, meaning it does not satisfy immutability. Key rotation has no effect on the data's deletion or overwrite status. Finally, Option D, applying a project-level constraint, has the same weakness as Option B: it sets a minimum, not a specific duration, for all buckets in a project. Only option A utilizes a locked retention policy on the specific bucket. This policy type achieves the exact level of immutability required.
Authoritative Links:
Cloud Storage Retention Policies: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/retention-policies Locking Retention Policies: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/locking-retention-policies Organizational Constraints: https://cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/organization- policy/understanding-constraints

question:
Your company has decided to make a major revision of their API in order to create better experiences for their developers. They need to keep the old version of the API available and deployable, while allowing new customers and testers to try out the new API. They want to keep the same SSL and DNS records in place to serve both APIs.
What should they do?
options: Configure a new load balancer for the new version of the API
Reconfigure old clients to use a new endpoint for the new API
Have the old API forward traffic to the new API based on the path
Use separate backend pools for each API path behind the load balancer
explanation:
The correct answer is D: Use separate backend pools for each API path behind the load balancer. This approach allows you to maintain both the old and new API versions simultaneously, under the same SSL and DNS records. By configuring a load balancer to route traffic based on the URL path, requests to the old API path will be directed to its corresponding backend pool, while requests to the new API path will be routed to the new API’s backend pool. This is achieved using path-based routing, a common technique in load balancing for managing different versions or functionalities of an application. Option A, while feasible, would require a different DNS record or SSL, which the requirements specifically avoid. Option B is not suitable because the goal is to support existing clients to the old API. Option C is inefficient as it puts a load on the old API. This ensures that existing clients using the old API are not disrupted and new clients or testers can easily access the new API without affecting the other.
Authoritative Links:
Google Cloud Load Balancing Overview: https://cloud.google.com/load-balancing/docs/load-balancing- overview URL Map Overview (for path-based routing): https://cloud.google.com/load-balancing/docs/url-map Backend Services and Backend Pools: https://cloud.google.com/load-balancing/docs/backend-service

question:
You have a Compute Engine application that you want to autoscale when total memory usage exceeds 80%. You have installed the Cloud Monitoring agent and configured the autoscaling policy as follows:
You observe that the application does not scale under high load. You want to resolve this. What should you do?
options: Change the Target type to DELTA_PER_MINUTE.
Change the Metric identifier to agent.googleapis.com/memory/bytes_used.
Change the filter to metric.label.state = ‘used’.
Change the filter to metric.label.state = ‘free’ and the Target utilization to 20.
explanation: C. Change the filter to metric.label.state = ‘used’. The current filter is set up with multiple AND conditions, which means it's looking for a metric that simultaneously has all these states: 'used', 'buffered', 'cached', and 'slab'. This is logically impossible, as a memory location can't be in multiple states at once. Therefore, the filter will never match any metrics, and the autoscaling policy won't trigger.

question: Your company has a Google Cloud project that uses BigOuery for data warehousing. The VPN tunnel between the on-premises environment and Google Cloud is configured with Cloud VPN. Your security team wants to avoid data exfiltration by malicious insiders, compromised code, and accidental oversharing. What should you do?
options: Configure Private Service Connect.
Configure VPC Service Controls and configure Private Google Access for on-promises hosts.
Create a service account, grant the BigQuery JobUser role and Storage Object Viewer role to the service account, and remove all other Identity and Access Management (IAM) access from the project.
Configure Private Google Access.
explanation:
The correct answer is B: Configure VPC Service Controls and configure Private Google Access for on- premises hosts. Here's a detailed justification:
Data exfiltration prevention requires a multi-layered approach, focusing on both network-level security and resource-level access controls. VPC Service Controls (VPC SC) allows you to define a security perimeter around Google Cloud resources, like BigQuery, limiting data access to authorized networks and identities. This mitigates the risk of unauthorized access, even if an attacker gains credentials. VPC SC implements context- aware access, only allowing requests from within the defined perimeter.https://cloud.google.com/vpc-service- controls/docs/overview Since the company's on-premises environment needs to access BigQuery, configuring Private Google Access for on-premises hosts is crucial. Private Google Access enables on-premises systems to communicate with Google Cloud services, including BigQuery, over a private network (e.g., the Cloud VPN tunnel), without traversing the public internet. This further reduces the attack surface and improves security. VPC SC and Private Google Access work in tandem to provide a comprehensive data exfiltration prevention solution.
Option A, Private Service Connect, is mainly for privately accessing services hosted in other VPC networks, not necessarily for controlling data exfiltration from your own project by internal actors. Option C, manipulating service account roles, while a good practice, is insufficient. Restricting access to only one service account doesn't prevent an attacker who compromises that service account from exfiltrating data within the allowed roles. Option D, Private Google Access, only secures the network connection. It does not enforce context-aware access controls to prevent data exfiltration by authorized but malicious users or compromised code. Hence, option B provides a much more comprehensive solution for the given scenario. VPC SC prevents data egress by enforcing perimeters, and Private Google Access gives secure private connectivity to BigQuery from on-prem.

question: You are managing a few hundred Ubuntu and Red Hat Enterprise Linux virtual machines running on Google Compute Engine (GCE). You need to patch the operating systems for those VMs periodically in a secure and scalable way. What should you do?
options: Create a Cloud Scheduler job to run a Bash script that securely connects to each VM and applies the patches.
Configure Config Sync, and install the Ops Agent on each VM. Schedule a patch job to apply patches on each VM.
Schedule a Cloud Build job, and use Cloud Deploy to run a patch job that applies patches on each VM.
Set up VM Manager, and install the OS Config agent on each VM. Schedule a patch job to apply patches on each VM.
explanation:
D — Set up VM Manager, and install the OS Config agent on each VM. Schedule a patch job to apply patches on each VM.
When managing hundreds of Ubuntu and RHEL VMs on Google Compute Engine, you need a solution that is:
Secure (no SSH key sprawl or custom scripts) Scalable (works across many VMs) Automated & manageable (centralized patching, reporting, and scheduling) Google Cloud provides a native service that does exactly this: VM Manager (OS Config).

question:
Your company uses Salesforce for customer relationship management (CRM). ServiceNow for IT service management, and a Cloud SQL database to store customer transaction data You need to seamlessly connect, map.
and transform data between these systems to ensure data consistency and enable real-time reporting. You want to follow Google-recommended practices. What should you do?
options: Leverage Workflows to connect the services and transform the data.
Leverage Application Integration to connect the services and transform the data.
Leverage Pub/Sub and BigQuery to connect the services and transform the data.
Leverage Pub/Sub and Datastream to connect the services and transform the data.
explanation:
B. Leverage Application Integration to connect the services and transform the data.
Google Cloud's Application Integration is a fully managed, no-code/low-code Integration-Platform-as-a- Service (iPaaS) solution designed specifically for integrating various business systems, such as CRM, ITSM, and databases.

question: You are designing the architecture for a global social media site on Google Cloud. The site serves dynamic API content, static assets such as CSS. JS. images, and user-uploaded videos for streaming. You need to minimize latency for all content types for users worldwide. What should you do? (Choose two.)
options: Use an external Application Load Balancer to serve your application APIs.
Use Cloud CDN to serve static assets of your application.
Use Media CDN to serve static assets of your application
Use Memorystore to serve your web application.
Use a multi-region Cloud Storage bucket to serve your entire web application.
explanation:
A. External Application Load Balancer .
It is globally distributed and uses Google’s premium global network Routes users to the nearest healthy backend Ideal for dynamic API traffic Supports HTTPS, TLS termination, and advanced routing This is the recommended front end for global, latency-sensitive web applications.
B. Cloud CDN .
Caches static assets (CSS, JS, images) at Google’s edge locations Reduces latency and backend load Integrates natively with the external Application Load Balancer Serves users from the closest edge point of presence

question: A Cloud Run service running your serverless application is unable to connect to an AlloyDB database created with default configurations. You need to troubleshoot and resolve the issue as quickly as possible. What should you do?
options: Verify that the Cloud Run service and AlloyDB instance are in the same region.
Enable Direct VPC egress for the Cloud Run service, and send traffic directly to a VPC.
Disable the default public IP address of the AlloyDB instance, and use the private IP address in the connection string.
Create a Cloud SQL instance, and migrate the AlloyDB database to PostgreSQL on Cloud SQL.
explanation:
B — Enable Direct VPC egress for the Cloud Run service, and send traffic directly to a VPC By default, a Cloud Run service:
Runs in a fully managed, serverless environment Does not have direct access to your VPC network Uses public egress unless configured otherwise Meanwhile, an AlloyDB instance created with default settings:
Uses a private IP Is accessible only from within a VPC This network mismatch prevents Cloud Run from connecting to AlloyDB.

question:
Your company is building containerized applications as part of their CI/CD pipeline. To improve the security and maintainability of the build process, you need to:
•Identify potential vulnerabilities within your container images.
•Generate verifiable metadata about the builds for auditing and compliance.
•Create a comprehensive inventory of your application’s dependencies What should you do?
options: Use Cloud Build to build container images, and then trigger Artifact Analysis on images pushed to Artifact Registry.
Use Cloud Build to build container images, trigger Binary Authorization, and use Cloud Asset Inventory for tracking and analysis.
Use Cloud Build to build container images, push the images to Artifact Registry, and use Security Command Center for tracking and analysis.
Use Cloud Build to build container images, trigger Binary Authorization, and use Security Command Center for tracking and analysis.
explanation:
A — Use Cloud Build to build container images, and then trigger Artifact Analysis on images pushed to Artifact Registry.
You need to address three specific goals in your CI/CD pipeline:
Identify potential vulnerabilities in container images Generate verifiable metadata for auditing and compliance Create a comprehensive inventory of application dependencies

question: Your team is running applications on a Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) cluster with a private endpoint. You've set up a Cloud Deploy pipeline, but deployments to the GKE cluster are failing. You need to resolve the issue. What should you do?
options: Use VPC Service Controls with Cloud Build Update the Cloud pipeline to use Cloud Build as its execution environment.
Create a Cloud Build private pool in the default VPC. Use Cloud Build to deploy the applications to the GKE cluster.
Create a Cloud Build private pool that is peered with the same VPC network as your GKE cluster. Update the Cloud Deploy pipeline to use this private pool as its execution environment.
Create a custom target in Cloud Deploy Update the deploy pipeline to use the custom target for the application deployment.
explanation:
C. Create a Cloud Build private pool that is peered with the same VPC network as your GKE cluster. Update the Cloud Deploy pipeline to use this private pool as its execution environment.
When a GKE cluster has a private endpoint, its control plane is only accessible from within its own Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) network or peered networks. The default execution environment for Cloud Deploy is a Google-managed, multi-tenant infrastructure which does not have access to your private VPC network.
To resolve this connectivity issue, you must configure a private execution

question:
You are planning to migrate your on-premises compute and SAP workloads to Google Cloud. You want to follow Google-recommended practices to quickly create a cost estimate for running these workloads in Google Cloud.
What should you do?
options: Leverage Cloud Asset Inventory to gather data and generate a cost estimate.
Use the Google Cloud pricing calculator, and input the estimated resource to generate a cost estimate.
Engage with a Google Cloud partner to perform a comprehensive assessment and provide a customized cost estimate.
Gather data about your current environment, and leverage Google Cloud Migration Center to generate a cost estimate.
explanation: Engage with a Google Cloud partner to perform a comprehensive assessment and provide a customized cost estimate.

question: Your organization has a significant amount of log data stored in Cloud Logging. The data engineering team is accustomed to using SQL for analysis and wants the ability to create insightful dashboards for visualizing log trends and patterns. You want to follow the recommendations of the Google Cloud Well-Architected Framework to provide a solution for the data engineering team. What should you do?
options: Create a log sink, and export the data to BigQuery using Pub/Sub. Run queries and visualize the data with Cloud Monitoring dashboards.
Enable log analytics and run queries in Cloud Monitoring. Visualize the data using Vertex AI workbench.
Enable log analytics and run queries in the linked log dataset in BigQuery. Visualize the data with Looker Studio dashboards.
Create a log sink, and export the data to a storage bucket. Create an external table in BigQuery for the data in the bucket. Run queries and visualize the data with Cloud Monitoring dashboards.
explanation:
Log Analytics: Google Cloud's Log Analytics feature, powered by BigQuery, allows you to analyze logs in place using standard SQL without needing to set up complex export pipelines for every query. The data remains in Cloud Logging buckets, which are then accessible as linked datasets in BigQuery.
SQL for Analysis: This directly addresses the data engineering team's requirement to use SQL for analysis, leveraging BigQuery's powerful, serverless analytics capabilities on petabyte-scale data.
Looker Studio for Visualization: Looker Studio (formerly Google Data Studio) has a native connector for BigQuery, making it the recommended tool for creating interactive dashboards and visualizing the analysis results.

question: You are designing the storage architecture for a financial analytics platform. The platform ingests and stores terabytes of transactional data daily, which is used for both real-time fraud detection and long-term historical analysis. Transaction data from the last 30 days must be accessible with very low latency for the fraud detection engine. Data older than 30 days is accessed infrequently for quarterly reports, where retrieval times of a few seconds are acceptable. All data must be retained for five years to meet compliance regulations. You need to design a solution as cost-effective as possible. What should you do?
options: Store all transaction data in a Cloud Storage bucket using the Standard storage class for the entire five-year retention period.
Ingest all data into BigQuery using time-partitioned tables, and rely on BigQuery’s automatic long-term storage pricing for data older than 90 days.
Configure a Cloud Storage bucket with an Object Lifecycle Management policy to transition data from the Standard class to the Archive class after 30 days.
