Draw an architecture diagram that shows how to move from a single MySQL database to a MySQL cluster.
Load 10 TB of analytics data from a previous game into a Cloud SQL instance, and run test queries against the full dataset to confirm that they complete successfully.
Integrate Cloud Armor to defend against possible SQL injection attacks in analytics files uploaded to Cloud Storage.
explanation:
Okay, let's break down why options A and B are the correct steps for Mountkirk Games' analytics migration based on the case study, and why the others are not ideal.
Justification for A: Evaluate the impact of migrating their current batch ETL code to Cloud Dataflow.
Mountkirk Games currently has batch ETL processes (extract, transform, load) for their analytics. Cloud Dataflow is Google Cloud's managed service for stream and batch data processing. Evaluating the impact of migrating to Dataflow is crucial because:
Scalability: Dataflow can handle the growing data volumes associated with their games far more effectively than a traditional, potentially on-premise batch setup.
Serverless: Dataflow is serverless, meaning less infrastructure management overhead for Mountkirk Games.
Cost Optimization: Dataflow's pay-per-use model can potentially reduce costs compared to maintaining their current infrastructure.
Transformation capabilities: Dataflow's powerful transformation engine can provide better data handling capabilities.
This makes option A a sound and necessary part of their migration plan.
Justification for B: Write a schema migration plan to denormalize data for better performance in BigQuery.
Mountkirk Games is migrating to BigQuery for analytics. BigQuery is a columnar data warehouse that excels at analyzing large datasets. Denormalizing data can significantly improve query performance in BigQuery:
Reduced Joins: Denormalization reduces the need for complex joins, which can be very expensive in large data sets.
Faster Reads: Fewer joins means faster access to the needed data, thereby improving query response times.
Optimized for Analytics: Denormalized data structures are optimized for read-heavy analytics workloads.
A well-defined schema migration plan that denormalizes data will be essential for the success of their migration.
Why other options are incorrect:
C: Draw an architecture diagram that shows how to move from a single MySQL database to a MySQL cluster. Mountkirk Games’ primary concern isn’t their transactional database, but rather their analytics.
Option C does not address their analytical database needs.
D: Load 10 TB of analytics data from a previous game into a Cloud SQL instance, and run test queries against the full dataset to confirm that they complete successfully. Cloud SQL is a relational database service. BigQuery is a better choice for their analytics workload (and indicated in the case study), and loading 10TB into SQL does not align with their goal of migrating to a scalable, more efficient model.
E: Integrate Cloud Armor to defend against possible SQL injection attacks in analytics files uploaded to Cloud Storage. While security is important, SQL injection is not applicable when files are uploaded to Cloud Storage. Also, their existing SQL based system is not where analytics will be residing. This option does not address the specific migration challenges they face for analytics.
Authoritative Links for Further Research:
Google Cloud Dataflow: https://cloud.google.com/dataflow Google BigQuery: https://cloud.google.com/bigquery BigQuery Data Denormalization: https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/best-practices- performance#denormalizing_data Cloud SQL: https://cloud.google.com/sql Cloud Armor: https://cloud.google.com/armor Therefore, A and B are the most relevant steps to include in Mountkirk Games' migration plan, focusing on the core aspects of their analytics requirements on Google Cloud Platform.

question: For this question, refer to the Mountkirk Games case study. You need to analyze and define the technical architecture for the compute workloads for your company, Mountkirk Games. Considering the Mountkirk Games business and technical requirements, what should you do?
options: Create network load balancers. Use preemptible Compute Engine instances.
Create network load balancers. Use non-preemptible Compute Engine instances.
Create a global load balancer with managed instance groups and autoscaling policies. Use preemptible Compute Engine instances.
Create a global load balancer with managed instance groups and autoscaling policies. Use non-preemptible Compute Engine instances.
explanation:
The correct answer is D because it best aligns with Mountkirk Games' needs for a resilient and scalable gaming platform, as outlined in the case study. Global load balancing is crucial for distributing player traffic across multiple regions, ensuring low latency and high availability, which are critical for a seamless gaming experience. Managed Instance Groups (MIGs) facilitate autoscaling, allowing the infrastructure to dynamically adjust to varying player demand, ensuring consistent performance even during peak hours. Non- preemptible Compute Engine instances are essential as they are not subject to abrupt termination, providing the stability and predictability needed for hosting continuous game sessions. Preemptible instances, while cost-effective, are unsuitable for this scenario due to the potential for disruption during gameplay. Network load balancers (options A and B) lack the global reach necessary for a multi-region gaming platform, while option C introduces instability by relying on preemptible instances. Therefore, a global load balancer with MIGs and non-preemptible instances (option D) offers the most reliable, scalable, and performant solution for Mountkirk Games.
Supporting concepts and links:
Global Load Balancer: Distributes traffic across multiple regions, improving availability and reducing latency.https://cloud.google.com/load-balancing/docs/load-balancing-concepts Managed Instance Groups (MIGs): Enable autoscaling and self-healing of virtual machine instances.https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/instance-groups Non-Preemptible Compute Engine Instances: Provide stable compute capacity with no risk of preemption.https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/instances/preemptible Autoscaling: Automatically adjusts resources based on demand, optimizing costs and performance.https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/autoscaling

question: For this question, refer to the Mountkirk Games case study. Mountkirk Games wants to design their solution for the future in order to take advantage of cloud and technology improvements as they become available. Which two steps should they take? (Choose two.)
options: Store as much analytics and game activity data as financially feasible today so it can be used to train machine learning models to predict user behavior in the future.
Begin packaging their game backend artifacts in container images and running them on Google Kubernetes Engine to improve the ability to scale up or down based on game activity.
Set up a CI/CD pipeline using Jenkins and Spinnaker to automate canary deployments and improve development velocity.
Adopt a schema versioning tool to reduce downtime when adding new game features that require storing additional player data in the database.
Implement a weekly rolling maintenance process for the Linux virtual machines so they can apply critical kernel patches and package updates and reduce the risk of 0-day vulnerabilities.
explanation:
The correct answer is AB. Option A aligns with future-proofing by leveraging data for future AI/ML advancements. Storing vast amounts of analytics and game activity data today allows Mountkirk Games to train sophisticated machine learning models in the future. These models can predict user behavior, personalize gameplay, and improve engagement, becoming a strategic asset as technology progresses.
Option B, adopting containerization with Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE), enables greater flexibility and scalability. Containerizing game backend artifacts and running them on GKE improves the ability to scale resources up or down efficiently based on game activity fluctuations. This is key for cost-effectiveness and adaptability, vital for handling unforeseen growth or changes in player behavior. Containerization facilitates the adoption of new microservices or architectural patterns in the future, providing agility.
Option C, setting up a CI/CD pipeline, is beneficial but primarily optimizes the current development process, not specifically preparing them for future technology advancements. While important, it's not directly related to taking advantage of future cloud improvements. Similarly, Option D, schema versioning, reduces downtime but does not enable capitalizing on new cloud features in the future. It solves database management issues for the current setup and does not align with their long-term vision of adapting to cloud and technology advancements. Option E is a security-related measure that does not relate to future technology improvements, it is more focused on maintaining the present infrastructure rather than adapting to the future.
Therefore, AB directly addresses Mountkirk Games' goal of future-proofing by ensuring they have the data to fuel future AI/ML initiatives and an infrastructure capable of scaling based on evolving demands.
Authoritative Links:
Google Kubernetes Engine: https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine Machine Learning on Google Cloud: https://cloud.google.com/products/ai Containerization: https://www.docker.com/resources/what-container/

question: For this question, refer to the Mountkirk Games case study. Mountkirk Games wants you to design a way to test the analytics platform's resilience to changes in mobile network latency. What should you do?
options: Deploy failure injection software to the game analytics platform that can inject additional latency to mobile client analytics traffic.

Build a test client that can be run from a mobile phone emulator on a Compute Engine virtual machine, and run multiple copies in Google Cloud Platform regions all over the world to generate realistic traffic.
Add the ability to introduce a random amount of delay before beginning to process analytics files uploaded from mobile devices.
Create an opt-in beta of the game that runs on players' mobile devices and collects response times from analytics endpoints running in Google Cloud Platform regions all over the world.
explanation:
Option A, deploying failure injection software, is the most suitable approach because it directly addresses the requirement of testing resilience to network latency changes. This technique allows for controlled and simulated alterations in network conditions, enabling Mountkirk Games to observe the platform's behavior under various latency levels. Failure injection is a common practice in chaos engineering, designed to test system robustness. By injecting latency specifically into mobile client analytics traffic, the impact on the analytics pipeline can be directly assessed without requiring real users or extensive infrastructure. The other options are less efficient and relevant. Option B requires substantial setup and may not accurately reflect real-world network fluctuations. Option C introduces delays within the platform's processing logic rather than simulating network conditions. Option D relies on actual users and does not offer the same level of control or testing scope as option A.
In summary, failure injection software offers a controlled, targeted, and efficient method for testing a system's resilience to network latency variations, making it the ideal choice for Mountkirk Games. This approach aligns with best practices for assessing platform robustness in a cloud environment.
Authoritative Links:
Chaos Engineering: https://principlesofchaos.org/ Failure Injection: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/whitepapers/latest/chaos-engineering/failure-injection.html

question: For this question, refer to the Mountkirk Games case study. You need to analyze and define the technical architecture for the database workloads for your company, Mountkirk Games. Considering the business and technical requirements, what should you do?
options: Use Cloud SQL for time series data, and use Cloud Bigtable for historical data queries.
Use Cloud SQL to replace MySQL, and use Cloud Spanner for historical data queries.
Use Cloud Bigtable to replace MySQL, and use BigQuery for historical data queries.
Use Cloud Bigtable for time series data, use Cloud Spanner for transactional data, and use BigQuery for historical data queries.
explanation:
The correct answer is D because it aligns best with the data characteristics and usage patterns described in the Mountkirk Games case study. Option A is incorrect because Cloud SQL is not optimized for handling high volumes of time-series data, and Cloud Bigtable is better suited for storing and quickly querying this type of information. Option B is inadequate because while Cloud SQL can replace MySQL, Cloud Spanner is typically an overkill for simple historical data analysis that can be better handled by BigQuery. Option C uses Cloud Bigtable for transactional workloads, which Cloud Spanner is designed to handle, and utilizes BigQuery for historical data which is correct but incorrectly combines the other two database technologies.
Option D correctly leverages the strengths of each database service. Cloud Bigtable excels at storing and querying time-series data like game event logs due to its scalability and low latency read/write capabilities.
Cloud Spanner provides strong consistency and global transactions for transactional data such as player profiles and in-game purchases. Lastly, BigQuery is ideal for historical data analysis and reporting with its ability to efficiently process large datasets and perform ad-hoc queries. This architecture ensures that the company can handle its real-time operational needs efficiently, while having a powerful analysis tool.
Therefore, by using Cloud Bigtable for time-series, Cloud Spanner for transactional data, and BigQuery for historical analysis, Mountkirk Games ensures scalability, reliability, and performance while maximizing cost efficiency.
Authoritative Links:
Cloud Bigtable: https://cloud.google.com/bigtable - Documentation about its suitability for time-series and large datasets.
Cloud Spanner: https://cloud.google.com/spanner - Documentation on its features for globally distributed, strongly consistent transactions.
BigQuery: https://cloud.google.com/bigquery - Documentation showcasing its abilities for analytics and large dataset analysis.
Mountkirk Games Case Study: (Refer to the original exam materials for the case study details) - The case study should give context for the requirements and constraints.

question: For this question, refer to the Mountkirk Games case study. Which managed storage option meets Mountkirk's technical requirement for storing game activity in a time series database service?
options: Cloud Bigtable
Cloud Spanner
BigQuery
Cloud Datastore
explanation:
The correct answer is A. Cloud Bigtable. Mountkirk Games requires a time-series database service for storing game activity data. Cloud Bigtable is a highly scalable, fully managed, NoSQL wide-column database service well-suited for time-series data due to its efficient storage and retrieval of large datasets indexed by timestamps. Its ability to handle high write throughput and low-latency reads makes it ideal for ingesting and querying game events in real-time. While Cloud Spanner offers strong consistency and global scale, it is not optimized for time-series data retrieval like Bigtable. BigQuery is primarily an analytical data warehouse, not suitable for real-time operational workloads, and Cloud Datastore is more appropriate for application data than time-series use cases. Bigtable’s inherent structure, with rows keyed by time, allows for efficient filtering and retrieval of temporal data. It excels in situations requiring fast access to data ordered by time, such as tracking user actions or game state changes. The case study mentions the need for high volume data collection, which Bigtable is architected to manage effectively.
Supporting Links:
Cloud Bigtable Documentation: https://cloud.google.com/bigtable/docs Time-series Data with Cloud Bigtable: https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/databases/time-series-data- with-cloud-bigtable Choosing a Storage Option: https://cloud.google.com/learn/what-storage-option-should-you-use-on-google- cloud

question:
For this question, refer to the Mountkirk Games case study. You are in charge of the new Game Backend Platform architecture. The game communicates with the backend over a REST API.
You want to follow Google-recommended practices. How should you design the backend?
options: Create an instance template for the backend. For every region, deploy it on a multi-zone managed instance group. Use an L4 load balancer.
Create an instance template for the backend. For every region, deploy it on a single-zone managed instance group. Use an L4 load balancer.
Create an instance template for the backend. For every region, deploy it on a multi-zone managed instance group. Use an L7 load balancer.
Create an instance template for the backend. For every region, deploy it on a single-zone managed instance group. Use an L7 load balancer.
explanation:
The correct answer is C. The Mountkirk Games case study highlights the need for a scalable and resilient gaming backend. Deploying the backend on multi-zone managed instance groups (MIGs) ensures high availability and fault tolerance by distributing instances across multiple availability zones within a region. This safeguards against zone-level failures, preventing a single point of failure. Using a multi-zone MIG aligns with Google's recommended practices for building resilient architectures. An L7 load balancer, as opposed to an L4 load balancer, is crucial because the game communicates with the backend over a REST API. L7 load balancers operate at the application layer (HTTP/HTTPS), allowing for sophisticated routing, path-based routing, and the ability to inspect HTTP headers, which is essential for directing API requests effectively and potentially enabling features like session affinity. L4 load balancers operate at the transport layer (TCP/UDP), providing simpler load balancing based on IP addresses and ports. Given the REST API requirement, L7 functionality is needed. Therefore, creating an instance template, deploying on multi-zone MIGs, and using an L7 load balancer provides a highly available, scalable, and intelligent backend, consistent with Google Cloud's best practices for application development. Option C encapsulates best practices for high availability, scalability and layer 7 routing.
Supporting Links:
Managed Instance Groups (MIGs): https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/instance-groups Load Balancing Overview: https://cloud.google.com/load-balancing/docs/load-balancing-overview Choosing a Load Balancer: https://cloud.google.com/load-balancing/docs/choosing-load-balancer L4 vs. L7 Load Balancing: https://www.imperva.com/blog/layer-4-vs-layer-7-load-balancing/

question: You need to optimize batch file transfers into Cloud Storage for Mountkirk Games' new Google Cloud solution. The batch files contain game statistics that need to be staged in Cloud Storage and be processed by an extract transform load (ETL) tool. What should you do?
options: Use gsutil to batch move files in sequence.
Use gsutil to batch copy the files in parallel.
Use gsutil to extract the files as the first part of ETL.
Use gsutil to load the files as the last part of ETL.
explanation:
The optimal approach for transferring batch files to Cloud Storage for Mountkirk Games, given the need for staging before ETL processing, is to use gsutil to copy the files in parallel. gsutil is the command-line tool for Cloud Storage and its parallel functionality significantly accelerates large file transfers. Option B is correct because it leverages the concurrency of gsutil to maximize the bandwidth usage and minimize transfer times, unlike Option A which moves files sequentially. Extracting (Option C) or loading (Option D) within the ETL process are subsequent steps, and not the most efficient way to move files into staging, while copying is the fastest way to get the files into the stage bucket for ETL. Efficient data staging through parallel transfers is essential for minimizing the time required to make the data available for subsequent ETL operations. This is crucial for large batch transfers that would benefit significantly from parallel operations. Using gsutil with the -m flag enables the tool to copy data in a highly parallelized and efficient way.For more information, refer to the official Google Cloud documentation on gsutil:
gsutil cp documentation Parallel composite uploads using gsutil Google Cloud Storage Best Practices

question:
You are implementing Firestore for Mountkirk Games. Mountkirk Games wants to give a new game programmatic access to a legacy game's Firestore database.
Access should be as restricted as possible. What should you do?
options: Create a service account (SA) in the legacy game's Google Cloud project, add a second SA in the new game's IAM page, and then give the Organization Admin role to both SAs.
Create a service account (SA) in the legacy game's Google Cloud project, give the SA the Organization Admin role, and then give it the Firebase Admin role in both projects.
Create a service account (SA) in the legacy game's Google Cloud project, add this SA in the new game's IAM page, and then give it the Firebase Admin role in both projects.
Create a service account (SA) in the legacy game's Google Cloud project, give it the Firebase Admin role, and then migrate the new game to the legacy game's project.
explanation:
The correct answer is C. Here's why:
Option C provides the most secure and least privileged approach. We create a service account (SA) within the legacy game's project, where the Firestore database resides. This SA will act as the authorized identity for the new game's access. By adding this service account to the new game's IAM page, we grant the new game's project visibility to this identity. Then, assigning the "Firebase Admin" role specifically to this service account within both projects grants the necessary permissions to access and manage Firestore in the legacy game's database from the new game. Crucially, this is scoped, least-privilege access.
Options A and B incorrectly use the "Organization Admin" role, which grants excessive and unnecessary permissions. It's against the principle of least privilege to grant such wide-ranging access when only Firestore access is needed. "Organization Admin" has too many capabilities and should be avoided. Option D suggests migrating the new game to the legacy project, which isn't always desirable or practical; it can create dependencies that are best avoided. Also, moving projects for just accessing a database is not the ideal approach.
By using a dedicated service account and granting only "Firebase Admin" role, Option C follows the best practice for service-to-service authentication and authorization in Google Cloud. This approach minimizes the attack surface and helps maintain a more secure environment.
Relevant Links:
Service Accounts: https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-accounts Firebase Admin Role: https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/permissions-reference#firebase IAM Best Practices: https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/best-practices-for-managing-iam Principle of Least Privilege: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Principle_of_least_privilege

question: Mountkirk Games wants to limit the physical location of resources to their operating Google Cloud regions. What should you do?
options: Configure an organizational policy which constrains where resources can be deployed.
Configure IAM conditions to limit what resources can be configured.
Configure the quotas for resources in the regions not being used to 0.
Configure a custom alert in Cloud Monitoring so you can disable resources as they are created in other regions.
explanation:
Option A, configuring an organizational policy to constrain resource deployment locations, is the correct approach. Google Cloud's organizational policies provide centralized control over cloud resources.
Specifically, the "constraints/gcp.resourceLocations" constraint allows administrators to define which Google Cloud regions are permitted for resource creation within an organization, folder, or project. This policy enforcement is proactive, preventing the accidental or intentional deployment of resources in non-approved locations from the outset. This approach is superior because it's preventative and system-level. IAM conditions (option B) are more focused on access control and permissions, not on the physical location of resource creation. Quota adjustments (option C) might limit resource quantities, but they don't prevent creation in specific regions. Monitoring alerts (option D) are reactive; they flag issues after they occur, rather than blocking them initially. Mountkirk Games needs a preventative solution, making option A the most effective and secure choice. By applying the organizational policy, Mountkirk Games ensures adherence to their resource location strategy across their entire Google Cloud environment.
Authoritative Links for further research:
Organizational Policy Service: https://cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/organization- policy/overview Understanding constraints: https://cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/organization- policy/understanding-constraints List of constraints: https://cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/organization-policy/org-policy- constraints (Specifically, search for constraints/gcp.resourceLocations)

question: You need to implement a network ingress for a new game that meets the defined business and technical requirements. Mountkirk Games wants each regional game instance to be located in multiple Google Cloud regions. What should you do?
options: Configure a global load balancer connected to a managed instance group running Compute Engine instances.
Configure kubemci with a global load balancer and Google Kubernetes Engine.
Configure a global load balancer with Google Kubernetes Engine.
Configure Ingress for Anthos with a global load balancer and Google Kubernetes Engine.
explanation:
The correct answer is D. Configure Ingress for Anthos with a global load balancer and Google Kubernetes Engine. Here's why:
The requirement is to have a network ingress for a game deployed across multiple Google Cloud regions. This necessitates a global load balancer to distribute traffic across these regions. Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) is a suitable platform for deploying containerized game instances, offering scalability and orchestration. While options A, B, and C involve load balancing and GKE or Compute Engine, they don't fully address the multi-region deployment with a high level of control and management suitable for complex game architecture.
Option A suggests a global load balancer with Compute Engine instances. While functional for web applications, it lacks the container orchestration benefits of Kubernetes, making it less suitable for a game’s scaling needs. Option B, kubemci (Kubernetes Multi-Cluster Ingress), is a component typically used for managing ingress across multiple Kubernetes clusters, but it doesn't directly integrate with GKE in an optimized fashion the way Anthos does. Option C, a global load balancer with GKE, is a valid approach for global load balancing, but it's less optimal than option D when dealing with multi-cluster deployment and advanced traffic management use cases.
Anthos is Google's platform for multi-cloud and hybrid deployments. Anthos Ingress is specifically designed to handle traffic routing and management across multiple GKE clusters located in different regions, and it seamlessly works with a global load balancer. This provides sophisticated features for traffic splitting, failover, and canary deployments across the regions where game instances are located, fulfilling the multi- regional deployment and high-availability aspect of the requirement. The built-in integration with the load balancer and multi-cluster features of Anthos Ingress make it the most suitable and scalable solution for Mountkirk Game's needs.
Authoritative Links:
Anthos Ingress: https://cloud.google.com/anthos/docs/concepts/ingress-overview Global Load Balancing: https://cloud.google.com/load-balancing/docs/load-balancing-concepts Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE): https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs

question: Your development teams release new versions of games running on Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) daily. You want to create service level indicators (SLIs) to evaluate the quality of the new versions from the user's perspective. What should you do?
options: Create CPU Utilization and Request Latency as service level indicators.
Create GKE CPU Utilization and Memory Utilization as service level indicators.
Create Request Latency and Error Rate as service level indicators.
Create Server Uptime and Error Rate as service level indicators.
explanation:
The correct answer is C: Create Request Latency and Error Rate as service level indicators.
SLIs (Service Level Indicators) are metrics that directly reflect the user experience of a service. In the context of a game application, user perception is primarily affected by how quickly the game responds to their actions (latency) and how often they encounter errors. Option C captures these crucial aspects.
Request Latency: Measures the time it takes for the system to respond to a user action (e.g., button press, game update request). High latency directly translates to a poor user experience due to lag or delays. This is a key performance indicator that directly impacts the user's perception of responsiveness.
Error Rate: Tracks the percentage of failed requests or actions. High error rates indicate instability and functional issues, directly impacting the user's ability to play the game or progress, leading to frustration and churn.
Options A, B, and D are less effective as they focus on infrastructure metrics rather than the user's experience.
CPU Utilization and Memory Utilization (Options A and B): These metrics, while important for monitoring resource usage, do not directly translate to the user's experience. High CPU utilization might not always result in poor user experience if the service is handling requests efficiently. Conversely, low CPU usage might not guarantee a good experience if response times are slow. Infrastructure resources are important but not a direct indicator of the user experience.
Server Uptime (Option D): While server uptime is critical for service availability, it doesn't indicate how well the application is performing from a user's point of view. A server can be up, but the application could be responding slowly or failing. Uptime focuses on the infrastructure availability not application performance.
Therefore, for user-centric evaluation of game releases, latency and error rates are the most appropriate SLIs, as they closely represent the quality of experience from a user perspective. These metrics directly map to common user-impacting issues.
Supporting Links:
Google Cloud Documentation on Service Level Objectives (SLOs) and SLIs:
Google Cloud Documentation on monitoring with Google Cloud operations:
SRE book on monitoring: https://sre.google/sre-book/monitoring-distributed-systems/

question:
Mountkirk Games wants you to secure the connectivity from the new gaming application platform to Google Cloud.
You want to streamline the process and follow Google-recommended practices. What should you do?
options: Configure Workload Identity and service accounts to be used by the application platform.
Use Kubernetes Secrets, which are obfuscated by default. Configure these Secrets to be used by the application platform.

Configure Kubernetes Secrets to store the secret, enable Application-Layer Secrets Encryption, and use Cloud Key Management Service (Cloud KMS) to manage the encryption keys. Configure these Secrets to be used by the application platform.

Configure HashiCorp Vault on Compute Engine, and use customer managed encryption keys and Cloud Key Management Service (Cloud KMS) to manage the encryption keys. Configure these Secrets to be used by the application platform.
explanation:
The correct answer is A. Workload Identity is the Google-recommended approach for applications running on Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) or other Google Cloud compute platforms to securely access Google Cloud services. Instead of relying on static service account keys, Workload Identity allows applications to assume the identity of a Kubernetes service account, which is then mapped to a Google service account. This significantly enhances security by eliminating the need to store long-lived credentials within the application's environment or configuration. Option B is not ideal because Kubernetes Secrets, while offering some obfuscation, are not inherently secure for highly sensitive credentials and don't use Google's preferred approach. Option C adds a layer of encryption, but managing Kubernetes Secrets for such secrets is complex compared to Workload Identity. Option D is overly complex and introduces a third-party solution (HashiCorp Vault), which is unnecessary given the availability of a Google-native solution, Workload Identity. Using Workload Identity streamlines the credential management process, reduces the attack surface, and adheres to Google's best practices for securing application connectivity to Google Cloud services. This also promotes a least-privilege access model, since the application will only be able to access resources allowed by the mapped service account's permissions.
Supporting concepts:
Workload Identity: Allows Kubernetes service accounts to act as Google service accounts, offering improved security and manageability. https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/workload-identity Principle of Least Privilege: Granting only the minimum required access to perform necessary tasks.
Service Accounts: Special Google accounts that can be used by applications and virtual machines to access Google APIs.
Best Practices: Adhering to Google’s documented and recommended approaches for security and cloud operations.

question: Your development team has created a mobile game app. You want to test the new mobile app on Android and iOS devices with a variety of configurations. You need to ensure that testing is efficient and cost-effective. What should you do?
options: Upload your mobile app to the Firebase Test Lab, and test the mobile app on Android and iOS devices.
Create Android and iOS VMs on Google Cloud, install the mobile app on the VMs, and test the mobile app.
Create Android and iOS containers on Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE), install the mobile app on the containers, and test the mobile app.
Upload your mobile app with different configurations to Firebase Hosting and test each configuration.
explanation:
The most efficient and cost-effective approach for testing a mobile game app across various Android and iOS configurations is using Firebase Test Lab. Option A, utilizing Firebase Test Lab, is the correct answer because it offers a managed, cloud-based service specifically designed for testing mobile apps on a wide range of real and virtual devices. This eliminates the need for manual setup and maintenance of physical devices or virtual machines. It automates testing across multiple device types, operating systems, and screen resolutions, significantly reducing testing effort and time. Firebase Test Lab also provides detailed reports and analytics, facilitating quick identification and resolution of issues. Options B and C involve managing infrastructure (VMs or containers), leading to higher operational overhead and costs compared to the fully managed Firebase service. Option D, while related to Firebase, is for hosting and doesn't fulfill the requirement of diverse device testing. Firebase Test Lab optimizes resource utilization by only using the required devices, resulting in lower expenses than maintaining dedicated VMs. Therefore, Firebase Test Lab aligns perfectly with the requirements for efficiency and cost-effectiveness in testing mobile applications, making Option A the optimal solution.
Authoritative Links:
Firebase Test Lab Documentation: https://firebase.google.com/docs/test-lab Overview of Firebase Test Lab: https://cloud.google.com/test-lab/

question:
The TerramEarth development team wants to create an API to meet the company's business requirements. You want the development team to focus their development effort on business value versus creating a custom framework.
Which method should they use?
options: Use Google App Engine with Google Cloud Endpoints. Focus on an API for dealers and partners
Use Google App Engine with a JAX-RS Jersey Java-based framework. Focus on an API for the public
Use Google App Engine with the Swagger (Open API Specification) framework. Focus on an API for the public
Use Google Container Engine with a Django Python container. Focus on an API for the public
Use Google Container Engine with a Tomcat container with the Swagger (Open API Specification) framework. Focus on an API for dealers and partners
explanation:
The correct answer is A. Use Google App Engine with Google Cloud Endpoints. Focus on an API for dealers and partners.
Here's why:
Google App Engine is a fully managed, serverless platform, enabling developers to focus on code rather than infrastructure management. This directly addresses the requirement to minimize custom framework creation and prioritize business logic development. Google Cloud Endpoints provides a robust API management layer on top of App Engine. Endpoints handles authentication, authorization, API discovery, and other common concerns, further reducing the development team's overhead.
Options B and C, while utilizing App Engine, introduce external frameworks (JAX-RS Jersey and Swagger), which conflicts with the goal of minimizing custom frameworks. These options require additional configuration and learning, pulling focus away from core business value.
Options D and E suggest using Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE), a container orchestration service. While powerful, GKE introduces a higher level of complexity and overhead compared to App Engine. Managing containers and Kubernetes is outside the scope of focusing on business logic. Additionally, GKE's Django and Tomcat containers introduce specific frameworks, again, contrary to the requirement of minimizing custom frameworks.
Furthermore, the specific focus on an API for dealers and partners mentioned in option A aligns well with the benefits of managed services like Endpoints which provide features like authentication and authorization, often essential when working with partners.
In summary, option A offers the simplest and most effective approach by leveraging the serverless nature of App Engine and the robust API management capabilities of Cloud Endpoints, allowing the development team to dedicate their efforts to crafting the business logic of the API instead of infrastructure and framework maintenance.
Authoritative Links:
Google App Engine: https://cloud.google.com/appengine Google Cloud Endpoints: https://cloud.google.com/endpoints Serverless Computing: https://cloud.google.com/serverless

question:
Your development team has created a structured API to retrieve vehicle data. They want to allow third parties to develop tools for dealerships that use this vehicle event data. You want to support delegated authorization against this data.
What should you do?
options: Build or leverage an OAuth-compatible access control system
Build SAML 2.0 SSO compatibility into your authentication system
Restrict data access based on the source IP address of the partner systems
Create secondary credentials for each dealer that can be given to the trusted third party
explanation:
The correct answer is A, building or leveraging an OAuth-compatible access control system. OAuth 2.0 is the industry-standard protocol for delegated authorization, perfectly suited for the scenario described. It allows third-party applications to access resources on behalf of a user (in this case, a dealership) without needing their direct credentials. This is achieved by issuing access tokens to the third-party app, which it then uses to make API calls. Option B, SAML 2.0, is primarily designed for single sign-on (SSO) and focuses on user authentication, not authorization delegated to third parties. Option C, restricting by source IP, is a brittle and unscalable approach, unsuitable for dynamic cloud environments and would be hard to manage as third-party IP addresses are not easily known. Option D, secondary credentials, presents security risks by distributing long-term credentials and makes managing user access difficult. OAuth provides fine-grained control over what data a third party can access, allowing for scopes (specific permissions). It is significantly more secure and flexible than the other options. By using OAuth, the vehicle API can remain secure while enabling third- party innovation. OAuth offers features like token expiry, refresh tokens, and granular control over authorization scopes. This is also the best practice for modern API development and integration.
Further research:
OAuth 2.0 Official Website: https://oauth.net/2/ Google Cloud Documentation on OAuth 2.0: https://cloud.google.com/docs/security/oauth2 RFC 6749 - The OAuth 2.0 Authorization Framework: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc6749

question:
TerramEarth plans to connect all 20 million vehicles in the field to the cloud. This increases the volume to 20 million 600 byte records a second for 40 TB an hour.
How should you design the data ingestion?
options: Vehicles write data directly to GCS
Vehicles write data directly to Google Cloud Pub/Sub
Vehicles stream data directly to Google BigQuery
Vehicles continue to write data using the existing system (FTP)
explanation:
The correct answer is B: Vehicles write data directly to Google Cloud Pub/Sub. Here's why:
Google Cloud Pub/Sub is a highly scalable and reliable messaging service designed for ingesting high- volume, real-time data streams. Given the requirement of handling 20 million 600-byte records per second, amounting to 40 TB an hour, Pub/Sub's capabilities are perfectly suited for this data ingestion scenario. It acts as a decoupling layer, allowing vehicles (publishers) to send data without needing to know about downstream systems (subscribers).
Option A, writing directly to GCS, is not ideal for real-time ingestion. GCS is better suited for storage of large files, and while data could be ingested that way, it would not be as efficient nor as fast as Pub/Sub. Option C, streaming directly to BigQuery, might work eventually, but BigQuery is optimized for analytical workloads, not ingestion. Attempting to ingest directly at this volume may cause performance issues. Option D, using the existing FTP system, is not scalable nor designed for real-time data ingestion. This will introduce a single point of failure and cause bottle necks for the massive amount of data.
Pub/Sub's publish-subscribe model ensures data durability and high availability. Messages are stored redundantly, and subscribers can process them asynchronously, which is essential for handling large-scale data streams without overloading the downstream systems. This decoupling allows for independent scaling of both data producers (vehicles) and data consumers (processing applications).
Furthermore, Pub/Sub allows for fan-out scenarios where multiple applications can subscribe to the same data stream, enabling diverse processing needs. In this context, Pub/Sub offers superior flexibility and scalability compared to the other options.
For further reading, you can explore these links:
Google Cloud Pub/Sub Overview: https://cloud.google.com/pubsub/docs/overview Pub/Sub Use Cases: https://cloud.google.com/pubsub/docs/use-cases Introduction to Data Streaming: https://cloud.google.com/solutions/data-streaming

question:
You analyzed TerramEarth's business requirement to reduce downtime, and found that they can achieve a majority of time saving by reducing customer's wait time for parts. You decided to focus on reduction of the 3 weeks aggregate reporting time.
Which modifications to the company's processes should you recommend?
options: Migrate from CSV to binary format, migrate from FTP to SFTP transport, and develop machine learning analysis of metrics
Migrate from FTP to streaming transport, migrate from CSV to binary format, and develop machine learning analysis of metrics
Increase fleet cellular connectivity to 80%, migrate from FTP to streaming transport, and develop machine learning analysis of metrics
Migrate from FTP to SFTP transport, develop machine learning analysis of metrics, and increase dealer local inventory by a fixed factor
explanation:
Okay, let's break down why option C is the best approach for reducing TerramEarth's 3-week aggregate reporting time, focusing on cloud-relevant concepts.
The core problem is the delay in receiving and processing data for reporting, leading to extended wait times for parts. The prompt identifies that focusing on the aggregate reporting time yields the biggest impact on downtime. Here's why C is the optimal solution:
Increased Fleet Cellular Connectivity (80%): This is vital for faster data transmission from the field.
Increased connectivity means data arrives more frequently and reliably, eliminating bottlenecks related to data transport. It reduces reliance on intermittent or delayed FTP batch transfers.
Migration from FTP to Streaming Transport: FTP is a batch-oriented protocol, meaning data is transferred in chunks at intervals. Streaming (e.g., using Pub/Sub or Kafka) provides continuous, real-time data flow. This drastically reduces delays between data generation and availability, a critical factor in lowering the 3-week report turnaround. This is key in a cloud architecture that favors real-time processing.
Development of Machine Learning Analysis: Machine learning can analyze incoming data streams to identify patterns, predict part needs, and optimize inventory management in near real-time. It allows proactive inventory management and avoids relying only on historical batch analyses. This ties into cloud-based data analytics and ML capabilities.
Option A includes binary format migration and SFTP, but doesn't tackle the batch transfer, a significant contributor to the 3-week delay. Option B misses increased cellular connectivity, which is essential for reliable, faster data transmission. Option D’s suggestion about increased local inventory, while seemingly logical, isn't the most impactful solution when real-time data processing can predict parts needs. Focus should be on reducing delays in the process and not increasing storage space.
Option C addresses the data transport bottleneck and provides the foundation for real-time data driven decision making, aligning with key cloud capabilities. It shifts TerramEarth from a delayed, historical reporting model to a near real-time predictive model.
Authoritative Links for Further Research:
Google Cloud Pub/Sub: https://cloud.google.com/pubsub/docs (For understanding streaming data transport) Google Cloud BigQuery: https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs (For analysis and ML capabilities) Real-Time Data Analytics: https://cloud.google.com/solutions/real-time-data-analytics (Provides the broader concept)

question: Which of TerramEarth's legacy enterprise processes will experience significant change as a result of increased Google Cloud Platform adoption?
options: Opex/capex allocation, LAN changes, capacity planning
Capacity planning, TCO calculations, opex/capex allocation
Capacity planning, utilization measurement, data center expansion
Data Center expansion, TCO calculations, utilization measurement
explanation:
The correct answer is B. Capacity planning, TCO calculations, opex/capex allocation. Increased adoption of Google Cloud Platform (GCP) fundamentally shifts how TerramEarth approaches these three areas. Firstly, capacity planning moves from predicting physical server needs to provisioning resources elastically in the cloud, demanding a new mindset focused on autoscaling and usage patterns rather than fixed hardware.
Secondly, Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) calculations transform from upfront hardware purchases and maintenance costs to pay-as-you-go services, necessitating analysis of consumption, reserved instances, and other cloud pricing models. Lastly, operational expenditure (opex)/capital expenditure (capex) allocation shifts from traditional capital investments in infrastructure to operational expenses based on resource consumption, requiring financial adjustments. Options A, C, and D are less directly impacted initially; while LAN changes might eventually occur, they are not core to the primary shift in operational approach. Similarly, while utilization measurement and data center expansion will be impacted, the more transformative changes will be in capacity planning, TCO and opex/capex allocation processes. These shifts require significant updates to established legacy processes, making B the most accurate answer. Cloud adoption forces a move away from traditional IT management to more modern and agile operational approaches.
Further Research:
Google Cloud Pricing: https://cloud.google.com/pricing Cloud Capacity Planning: https://www.bmc.com/blogs/cloud-capacity-planning/ Understanding TCO in the Cloud: https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/enterprise-strategy/understanding-tco-in- the-cloud/ CapEx vs OpEx Cloud: https://www.cloudzero.com/blog/capex-vs-opex-cloud

question:
To speed up data retrieval, more vehicles will be upgraded to cellular connections and be able to transmit data to the ETL process. The current FTP process is error-prone and restarts the data transfer from the start of the file when connections fail, which happens often. You want to improve the reliability of the solution and minimize data transfer time on the cellular connections.
What should you do?
options: Use one Google Container Engine cluster of FTP servers. Save the data to a Multi-Regional bucket. Run the ETL process using data in the bucket

Use multiple Google Container Engine clusters running FTP servers located in different regions. Save the data to Multi-Regional buckets in US, EU, and Asia. Run the ETL process using the data in the bucket
Directly transfer the files to different Google Cloud Multi-Regional Storage bucket locations in US, EU, and Asia using Google APIs over HTTP(S). Run the ETL process using the data in the bucket

Directly transfer the files to a different Google Cloud Regional Storage bucket location in US, EU, and Asia using Google APIs over HTTP(S). Run the ETL process to retrieve the data from each Regional bucket
explanation:
The most suitable solution is C: Directly transfer the files to different Google Cloud Multi-Regional Storage bucket locations in US, EU, and Asia using Google APIs over HTTP(S). Run the ETL process using the data in the bucket.
Here's why:
Reliability: Option C addresses the core issue of unreliable transfers with the current FTP setup. Using Google APIs over HTTPS provides inherent reliability through features like retries and checksum verification, making data transfers robust. Google Cloud Storage also offers high durability and availability.
Data Transfer Efficiency: Instead of FTP, which struggles with intermittent connections, transferring via Google APIs with HTTP(S) is more efficient, especially over cellular networks. These connections are better suited for handling network disruptions.
Minimize Transfer Time: Direct transfer to Cloud Storage avoids intermediate steps like staging files on FTP servers, streamlining the process. Additionally, HTTPS connections are more efficient than FTP.
Multi-Regional Storage: Option C proposes using multi-regional buckets, which means the data is replicated across multiple regions which is more available. This aligns with the need to store the data in multiple locations and improves access time from different geographical locations for the ETL process.
Direct Integration: Using Google APIs allows for direct integration of the data transfer process with Google Cloud Storage. It avoids managing and scaling an FTP infrastructure using Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE).
Options A and B introduce unnecessary complexity and potential bottlenecks with maintaining FTP servers.
Option D is less suitable because it uses regional buckets which do not offer multi regional replication and is not as optimal when the data will be consumed in different regions.
In Summary: Option C optimizes the data transfer process, ensures data reliability, minimizes transfer time by leveraging Google APIs and Cloud Storage's multi-regional capabilities, and avoids unnecessary infrastructure overhead.
Authoritative Links:
Google Cloud Storage Overview: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/overview Cloud Storage HTTP(S) API: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/json_api/v1 Multi-Regional Storage: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/locations

question:
TerramEarth's 20 million vehicles are scattered around the world. Based on the vehicle's location, its telemetry data is stored in a Google Cloud Storage (GCS) regional bucket (US, Europe, or Asia). The CTO has asked you to run a report on the raw telemetry data to determine why vehicles are breaking down after 100 K miles. You want to run this job on all the data.
What is the most cost-effective way to run this job?
options: Move all the data into 1 zone, then launch a Cloud Dataproc cluster to run the job
Move all the data into 1 region, then launch a Google Cloud Dataproc cluster to run the job
Launch a cluster in each region to preprocess and compress the raw data, then move the data into a multi- region bucket and use a Dataproc cluster to finish the job
Launch a cluster in each region to preprocess and compress the raw data, then move the data into a region bucket and use a Cloud Dataproc cluster to finish the job
explanation:
The most cost-effective solution is option D. The key challenge is processing data distributed across regional GCS buckets (US, Europe, Asia). Option D advocates for a distributed processing approach, launching a Cloud Dataproc cluster in each region where data resides. This avoids expensive data transfer by performing initial processing (like compression) locally, closer to the source. This pre-processing, including compression, reduces the volume of data that needs to be transferred, minimizing network egress charges. After preprocessing, the compressed data is moved to a single regional bucket. Finally, a single Cloud Dataproc cluster is launched to analyze the preprocessed, aggregated data. This consolidated cluster simplifies the final analysis stage and avoids the overhead of managing multiple analysis clusters. Option A and B propose moving all data into a single zone or region, which incurs substantial data egress costs as large datasets would have to be moved from their original location. This is particularly inefficient when the processing can be performed locally. Option C is similar to D in that it suggests pre-processing, but specifies moving to a multi- region bucket, which doesn't bring the benefit of local processing. While multi-region storage provides availability and lower latency reads to consumers across the regions, it isn't relevant for the processing in this case. The use of Cloud Dataproc is appropriate for running large-scale data processing jobs. The local clusters preprocess and compress the data, reducing cost and accelerating processing.
Further research:
Cloud Dataproc: Learn about Google's managed Hadoop and Spark service for large-scale data processing.
Cloud Storage Pricing: Understand network egress costs related to moving data between regions and zones.
Data Locality: Explore the concept of data locality in distributed computing for efficient data processing.

question:
TerramEarth has equipped all connected trucks with servers and sensors to collect telemetry data. Next year they want to use the data to train machine learning models. They want to store this data in the cloud while reducing costs.
What should they do?
options: Have the vehicle's computer compress the data in hourly snapshots, and store it in a Google Cloud Storage (GCS) Nearline bucket
Push the telemetry data in real-time to a streaming dataflow job that compresses the data, and store it in Google BigQuery
Push the telemetry data in real-time to a streaming dataflow job that compresses the data, and store it in Cloud Bigtable
Have the vehicle's computer compress the data in hourly snapshots, and store it in a GCS Coldline bucket
explanation:
The most cost-effective approach for TerramEarth is to compress telemetry data on the trucks and store it in Google Cloud Storage (GCS) Coldline. Option D directly addresses the need for cost reduction while ensuring data is available for future machine learning model training. Since the data is not immediately needed and is intended for later analysis, near-real-time storage is not required. Coldline storage is optimized for infrequent access, offering the lowest storage cost, which aligns with the given scenario. By compressing data on the trucks before upload, they can further minimize storage space and reduce costs. Options A and B involve more complex infrastructure and higher costs. Nearline storage (Option A) is more expensive than Coldline and not necessary for data accessed infrequently. Streaming dataflow to BigQuery (Option B) is suitable for real-time data analysis, adding unnecessary overhead and costs for this use case. Cloud Bigtable (Option C) is designed for large-scale, low-latency data access which is not necessary for the type of processing the customer intends for its data.
Key Concepts:
Google Cloud Storage (GCS): A scalable object storage service. (https://cloud.google.com/storage) GCS Storage Classes: GCS offers different storage classes (Standard, Nearline, Coldline, Archive) with varying costs and availability levels. (https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/storage-classes) Coldline: Designed for data accessed infrequently (once a quarter), offering the lowest storage cost.
Nearline: Designed for data accessed less frequently (once per month), offering lower storage cost compared to standard storage but more expensive than Coldline.
Data Compression: Reducing the size of data, lowering storage costs, and minimizing bandwidth usage during transfers.
Streaming Dataflow: A fully managed stream processing service used for real-time data transformation and analysis.
BigQuery: A serverless, highly scalable, data warehouse.
Bigtable: A scalable, fully managed NoSQL database service for low latency access to large datasets.
Therefore, option D provides the most cost-effective solution by compressing data on the edge and storing it in Coldline for later use in machine learning, which is aligned with the business requirements and best practices for cost optimization.

question:
Your agricultural division is experimenting with fully autonomous vehicles. You want your architecture to promote strong security during vehicle operation.
Which two architectures should you consider? (Choose two.)
options: Treat every micro service call between modules on the vehicle as untrusted.
Require IPv6 for connectivity to ensure a secure address space.
Use a trusted platform module (TPM) and verify firmware and binaries on boot.
Use a functional programming language to isolate code execution cycles.
Use multiple connectivity subsystems for redundancy.
Enclose the vehicle's drive electronics in a Faraday cage to isolate chips.
explanation:
Here's a justification for why options A and C are the best choices for securing autonomous vehicles, along with explanations and supporting links:
Option A: Treat every micro service call between modules on the vehicle as untrusted.
Autonomous vehicles are complex systems relying on numerous interconnected modules (e.g., sensor processing, steering control, navigation). Treating each communication between these microservices as potentially untrusted promotes a zero-trust security model. This means each component must verify the legitimacy and authorization of every request, irrespective of its origin within the vehicle. This approach helps mitigate risks associated with compromised components or malicious injection, limiting potential damage.
This practice is crucial for defense in depth, ensuring a multi-layered approach to security.
Option C: Use a trusted platform module (TPM) and verify firmware and binaries on boot.
A Trusted Platform Module (TPM) is a specialized chip designed to secure hardware by storing cryptographic keys and verifying boot processes. By using a TPM, the vehicle’s firmware and software binaries can be cryptographically measured and verified before they are executed. This ensures that only authorized and unmodified software can run, mitigating risks arising from malware or tampered code being loaded during the boot process. Verifying the boot process establishes a strong root of trust, which is foundational to a secure system.
Why the other options are less suitable:
Option B (Require IPv6): While IPv6 offers benefits like a larger address space, it doesn't inherently provide better security than IPv4. Security depends on proper implementation and configurations, not the IP protocol version itself.
Option D (Use functional programming language): While functional programming languages can contribute to code stability and reduce errors, they do not directly address the core security vulnerabilities that require hardware-level and system-wide security measures.
Option E (Use multiple connectivity subsystems for redundancy): Redundancy enhances availability and fault tolerance but doesn't address security directly. While important for resilience, it doesn't prevent security breaches.
Option F (Enclose the vehicle's drive electronics in a Faraday cage): While a Faraday cage might protect against external electromagnetic interference, it doesn't protect the system against internal software vulnerabilities, malware, or hardware compromise attacks.
Authoritative Links:
Zero Trust Security: https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/identity-security/zero-trust-beyondcorp-google (Google Cloud's approach to zero trust security) Trusted Platform Module (TPM): https://trustedcomputinggroup.org/resource/trusted-platform-module-tpm- 2-0-introduction/ (Trusted Computing Group's page on TPM) Defense in Depth: https://www.cisa.gov/news-events/news/defense-depth-concept (CISA's explanation of defense in depth) In conclusion, combining a zero-trust approach to inter-module communication (A) and a verified boot process using a TPM (C) creates a strong foundation for the secure operation of autonomous vehicles, addressing critical aspects of internal and boot-level security.

question:
Operational parameters such as oil pressure are adjustable on each of TerramEarth's vehicles to increase their efficiency, depending on their environmental conditions. Your primary goal is to increase the operating efficiency of all 20 million cellular and unconnected vehicles in the field.
How can you accomplish this goal?
options: Have you engineers inspect the data for patterns, and then create an algorithm with rules that make operational adjustments automatically
Capture all operating data, train machine learning models that identify ideal operations, and run locally to make operational adjustments automatically
Implement a Google Cloud Dataflow streaming job with a sliding window, and use Google Cloud Messaging (GCM) to make operational adjustments automatically
Capture all operating data, train machine learning models that identify ideal operations, and host in Google Cloud Machine Learning (ML) Platform to make operational adjustments automatically
explanation:
Option B is the most suitable solution because it directly addresses the core challenge of optimizing operational parameters for a large fleet of diverse, often disconnected vehicles. By capturing all operating data, machine learning models can be trained to understand the complex relationships between environmental conditions and optimal oil pressure settings. The critical aspect here is running these models locally on the vehicles. This avoids the pitfalls of relying on constant connectivity for adjustments, which is a key consideration given that many vehicles are unconnected. The vehicles can independently make optimized adjustments based on the trained model, ensuring efficiency even when offline. This approach leverages edge computing principles, distributing the intelligence to the edge devices (vehicles) for improved performance and reduced latency. Hosting the ML models locally also minimizes the need for high data transfer bandwidth, which would be impractical for 20 million vehicles. This distributed model ensures the continuous and optimized operation of each vehicle, resulting in an overall increase in fleet efficiency.
Why other options are less suitable:
Option A: While analyzing data patterns manually could provide insights, it is not scalable or efficient for 20 million vehicles. The manual creation of rules would be time-consuming and lack the adaptability of a machine-learning approach.
Option C: This approach is inappropriate because it assumes a continuous data stream and reliable connectivity for all vehicles. Using GCM for adjustments would fail for unconnected vehicles and would incur latency in connected vehicles. Dataflow is also not suited for real time adjustment on device since it is a batch processing platform and not an inference platform.
Option D: Hosting models in Google Cloud ML Platform and sending adjustments from the cloud suffers from the same connectivity and latency problems as option C. Furthermore, there would be a high cost involved in transferring operational data for 20 million vehicles to the cloud. Also there is the added issue of high latency.
Supporting links:
Edge Computing: https://cloud.google.com/solutions/edge-computing Machine Learning on Google Cloud: https://cloud.google.com/ml-engine

question: For this question, refer to the TerramEarth case study. To be compliant with European GDPR regulation, TerramEarth is required to delete data generated from its European customers after a period of 36 months when it contains personal data. In the new architecture, this data will be stored in both Cloud Storage and BigQuery. What should you do?
options: Create a BigQuery table for the European data, and set the table retention period to 36 months. For Cloud Storage, use gsutil to enable lifecycle management using a DELETE action with an Age condition of 36 months.

Create a BigQuery table for the European data, and set the table retention period to 36 months. For Cloud Storage, use gsutil to create a SetStorageClass to NONE action when with an Age condition of 36 months.

Create a BigQuery time-partitioned table for the European data, and set the partition expiration period to 36 months. For Cloud Storage, use gsutil to enable lifecycle management using a DELETE action with an Age condition of 36 months.

Create a BigQuery time-partitioned table for the European data, and set the partition expiration period to 36 months. For Cloud Storage, use gsutil to create a SetStorageClass to NONE action with an Age condition of 36 months.
explanation:
The correct answer is C. Here's a detailed justification:
BigQuery:
Time-partitioned tables: European GDPR mandates data deletion after 36 months. Using a time-partitioned table in BigQuery allows us to efficiently manage data based on its timestamp. Each partition represents a time segment (e.g., monthly). This approach enables targeted deletion of partitions older than 36 months, rather than needing to scan and delete individual rows, optimizing performance and reducing cost.
Partition Expiration: Setting the partition expiration period to 36 months ensures that entire partitions containing data older than 36 months are automatically removed. This enforces compliance with the GDPR requirement by automatically deleting data that has exceeded the retention period.
Cloud Storage:
Lifecycle Management: Cloud Storage offers lifecycle management rules, which provide automated actions based on predefined conditions.
DELETE Action: The DELETE action is used to remove objects from Cloud Storage. By setting the Age condition to 36 months, any object in the Cloud Storage bucket that is older than 36 months will be permanently deleted, thereby meeting the GDPR requirement for data erasure.
SetStorageClass to NONE is incorrect: The SetStorageClass action does not delete data. Setting it to NONE (which is not even a valid storage class) would have no effect on data retention. This makes options B and D incorrect. The correct action for deletion is DELETE.
Why other options are incorrect:
Options A and B do not use time partitioning which is efficient for dealing with time-based deletion requirements in BigQuery. Option A only uses table retention, which would mean entire tables have to be deleted after 36 months. Time-partitioning is a more efficient way to manage and delete the time-based data.
Options B and D attempt to use SetStorageClass to NONE for deletion which is incorrect. SetStorageClass is for changing a storage class of object not deletion.
Authoritative Links:
BigQuery Time-Partitioned Tables: https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/partitioned-tables Cloud Storage Object Lifecycle Management: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/lifecycle BigQuery Table Expiration: https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/managing-tables#table_expiration gsutil lifecycle command: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/gsutil/commands/lifecycle

question:
For this question, refer to the TerramEarth case study. TerramEarth has decided to store data files in Cloud Storage. You need to configure Cloud Storage lifecycle rule to store 1 year of data and minimize file storage cost.
Which two actions should you take?
options: Create a Cloud Storage lifecycle rule with Age: 30, Storage Class: Standard, and Action: Set to Coldline, and create a second GCS life-cycle rule with Age: 365, Storage Class: Coldline, and Action: Delete.

Create a Cloud Storage lifecycle rule with Age: 30, Storage Class: Coldline, and Action: Set to Nearline, and create a second GCS life-cycle rule with Age: 91, Storage Class: Coldline, and Action: Set to Nearline.

Create a Cloud Storage lifecycle rule with Age: 90, Storage Class: Standard, and Action: Set to Nearline, and create a second GCS life-cycle rule with Age: 91, Storage Class: Nearline, and Action: Set to Coldline.

Create a Cloud Storage lifecycle rule with Age: 30, Storage Class: Standard, and Action: Set to Coldline, and create a second GCS life-cycle rule with Age: 365, Storage Class: Nearline, and Action: Delete.
explanation:
Here's a justification for why option A is the correct approach for TerramEarth's data storage needs, focusing on minimizing cost while retaining data for one year:
Option A uses Cloud Storage lifecycle rules to move data through storage tiers based on age. The first rule moves files older than 30 days from the Standard tier to the Coldline tier. This transition significantly reduces storage costs, as Coldline storage is designed for infrequent access and is less expensive than Standard. The second rule, which deletes files older than 365 days, ensures that data is only kept for the required one year, further optimizing cost. It's important to understand that Google Cloud Storage lifecycle rules allow for automated transition of objects between storage classes based on defined criteria (e.g. age). Each storage class provides different cost and access patterns, which need to be aligned to use case. The Standard tier is ideal for frequently accessed data, while Nearline, Coldline, and Archive tiers progressively offer lower costs for progressively less frequently accessed data. Coldline is well-suited for data accessed less than once per quarter, so the proposed 30 day transition is a good fit for cost optimization.
Option B is incorrect because it would move the files in a way that increases the storage cost. Nearline is more expensive than Coldline and moving files to it after 30 days isn't efficient. Option C is also inefficient as it initially stores files in the Standard tier for 90 days and then to Nearline, which is more expensive than Coldline. Option D would store the data for a year in the Coldline tier then delete it, it has no transition to another more affordable tier and doesn't provide the most cost effective option.
Therefore, option A is correct as it achieves the goals set by the prompt, that is minimize file storage cost while storing files for 1 year.
Relevant Links:
Cloud Storage Lifecycle Management: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/lifecycle Cloud Storage Classes: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/storage-classes

question:
For this question, refer to the TerramEarth case study. You need to implement a reliable, scalable GCP solution for the data warehouse for your company, TerramEarth.
Considering the TerramEarth business and technical requirements, what should you do?
options: Replace the existing data warehouse with BigQuery. Use table partitioning.
Replace the existing data warehouse with a Compute Engine instance with 96 CPUs.
Replace the existing data warehouse with BigQuery. Use federated data sources.
Replace the existing data warehouse with a Compute Engine instance with 96 CPUs. Add an additional Compute Engine preemptible instance with 32 CPUs.
explanation:
The optimal solution for TerramEarth's data warehouse, as per the case study, is to replace the existing system with BigQuery and leverage table partitioning (Option A). BigQuery is a fully managed, serverless data warehouse service ideal for TerramEarth's needs of scalability, reliability, and cost-effectiveness. Unlike Compute Engine instances (Options B and D), BigQuery eliminates the operational overhead of managing servers, patching, and infrastructure scaling. BigQuery's architecture inherently handles large datasets, ensuring high availability and fault tolerance. Table partitioning, specifically, enhances query performance and reduces costs by limiting the amount of data scanned during queries. In contrast, using large Compute Engine instances (Options B and D) requires significant upfront capacity planning, leading to potential underutilization and higher costs. Preemptible instances (Option D) introduce instability, unsuitable for a reliable data warehouse. Federated data sources (Option C) are beneficial for querying external data but not as the core replacement for the entire data warehouse. TerramEarth's requirements, as per the case study, emphasizes the need for managed services rather than managing underlying infrastructure. Thus, BigQuery with table partitioning aligns perfectly.
For further research, refer to these official Google Cloud resources:
BigQuery Overview: https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/introduction BigQuery Table Partitioning: https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/partitioned-tables BigQuery Pricing: https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/pricing Compute Engine Overview: https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/

question:
For this question, refer to the TerramEarth case study. A new architecture that writes all incoming data to BigQuery has been introduced. You notice that the data is dirty, and want to ensure data quality on an automated daily basis while managing cost.
What should you do?
options: Set up a streaming Cloud Dataflow job, receiving data by the ingestion process. Clean the data in a Cloud Dataflow pipeline.
Create a Cloud Function that reads data from BigQuery and cleans it. Trigger the Cloud Function from a Compute Engine instance.
Create a SQL statement on the data in BigQuery, and save it as a view. Run the view daily, and save the result to a new table.
Use Cloud Dataprep and configure the BigQuery tables as the source. Schedule a daily job to clean the data.
explanation:
The best approach to address TerramEarth's dirty data issue in BigQuery, with a focus on automation, cost- effectiveness, and data quality, is option D, utilizing Cloud Dataprep.
Here's why:
Cloud Dataprep is specifically designed for data wrangling and cleaning. It offers a user-friendly interface for data profiling, transformation, and validation, without requiring extensive coding knowledge. This makes it easy to implement complex cleaning rules and transformations on BigQuery data. By connecting directly to BigQuery, it avoids the complexity and costs associated with moving data to another processing service for cleaning. Setting up a daily scheduled job within Cloud Dataprep handles the automated aspect of the requirement. It directly works on BigQuery tables as data source and enables users to run the clean data pipeline daily.Option A, while capable of data cleaning, involves setting up a more complex and resource- intensive Cloud Dataflow pipeline. It also requires a streaming data ingestion pipeline setup, which does not match the requirement of cleaning BigQuery stored data.Option B introduces unnecessary complexity by creating a Cloud Function triggered by a Compute Engine instance to clean data within BigQuery. This approach requires scripting data movement within BigQuery and may incur costs for both compute and data transfer operations. It is more difficult to manage and maintain compared to a purpose built data prep tool.Option C only creates a view which does not clean the underlying data. A view will only provide a query result.
Cloud Dataprep is the best solution because it's purpose-built for data wrangling, is user-friendly, cost- effective, and supports scheduled automation.
Authoritative links:
Cloud Dataprep Overview: https://cloud.google.com/dataprep/docs/concepts/overview Data Prep on BigQuery: https://cloud.google.com/dataprep/docs/how-to/connect-to-bq

question: For this question, refer to the TerramEarth case study. Considering the technical requirements, how should you reduce the unplanned vehicle downtime in GCP?
options: Use BigQuery as the data warehouse. Connect all vehicles to the network and stream data into BigQuery using Cloud Pub/Sub and Cloud Dataflow. Use Google Data Studio for analysis and reporting.

Use BigQuery as the data warehouse. Connect all vehicles to the network and upload gzip files to a Multi- Regional Cloud Storage bucket using gcloud. Use Google Data Studio for analysis and reporting.

Use Cloud Dataproc Hive as the data warehouse. Upload gzip files to a Multi-Regional Cloud Storage bucket. Upload this data into BigQuery using gcloud. Use Google Data Studio for analysis and reporting.
Use Cloud Dataproc Hive as the data warehouse. Directly stream data into partitioned Hive tables. Use Pig scripts to analyze data.
explanation:
Option A is the most suitable solution for reducing unplanned vehicle downtime, aligning with the technical requirements of the TerramEarth case study. Real-time data ingestion and analysis are crucial for proactive maintenance and minimizing downtime.
